summary: Fleet 2: per-namespace extraction-coverage audit (60) + judge-dispute re-adjudication (19) + synthesis + skeptics

extraction_findings.name: Relative-date / event-time resolution (dated events stamped with session time, never backdated)
extraction_findings.recommendation: The single most pervasive gap (53/60 audits, all 4 windows) and the top starvation source for temporal-reasoning questions. Add a deterministic write-time date resolver in the extraction path: whenever a claim or event mentions a relative or in-text date ('last June', 'two weeks ago', explicit calendar dates), resolve it against the record's session timestamp and store the RESOLVED date as the claim's event time (valid_at), keeping ingestion time as a separate observed_at field. Dual-timestamping (event_time vs mention_time) is benchmark-agnostic, needs no LLM for the common patterns, and also fixes the sub-cases seen here: undated events, unanchored future dates, and event locations/durations detached from their date.

extraction_findings.name: Typed numeric quantities (value+unit never parsed; decimals/currency truncated; spelled-out numbers missed)
extraction_findings.recommendation: Add a deterministic quantity typer that runs before sentence-splitting in eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py: tokenize numerals first so the shredder never splits on '.' inside a decimal (the corrupted-value bug in l-g10/al-g2/al-g4/l-g27), then emit typed quantity claims with {value, unit, subject} covering digits, spelled-out numbers, currency, ages, durations, and frequencies. This directly feeds count/aggregation questions — the exact family behind the known live LME-S numeric-aggregation failures (5/13 verified-wrong) — and also fixes degenerate value fields ('True', sentence-shaped values) seen in mo-g8@r15/l-g16.

extraction_findings.name: Zero graph-channel namespaces — consolidation extract-call budget starvation (single shared cause)
extraction_findings.recommendation: All 7 zero-edge namespaces (g0,g8,g11,g13,g17,g21,g25 in _r1_codex) share one proven cause: CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_CALL_BUDGET=90 + CONSOLIDATION_LONG_HAYSTACK_RAW_ONLY=1 made consolidate_pending (eidetic/engine.py ~1436-1530) collapse entire oversized batches to raw_only (window_allowance=0), so client.extract_edges never ran — 0 edges, 0 EventRecords, 0 entities, while budget-exempt heuristic claims_for_record kept firing (~5k sentence-shredded claims each, 74% junk subjects). This wipes EVERY edge/event-dependent question in 12% of audited namespaces, so it ranks above per-class gaps despite the low count. Fix the scheduler, not the extractor: guarantee a per-record minimum extraction window, carry unused budget across sleeps, and never zero out a whole batch — records still carry pending_consolidation=True, so a drain-and-retry loop on subsequent sleeps works. Immediate remediation: re-sleep these namespaces under BENCH_FULL_CONSOLIDATION_PROFILE (eidetic/config.py:87-98, budget=0).

extraction_findings.name: Entity-relationship edge extraction (person-person / person-thing graph coverage, including partial-starvation namespaces)
extraction_findings.recommendation: Make the graph channel budget-independent at the floor: add a deterministic pattern-based edge pass (kinship, possession, employment, alias/nickname, transfer-with-direction) that always runs alongside claim extraction, with LLM extract_edges as enrichment only; and emit an edge automatically whenever a typed relational claim is stored so claims and edges cannot diverge (mo-g8@r15 shows relation empty on 100% of claims). Caveat for prioritization: 7 of the 26 _r1 members are the budget-starvation namespaces and several more are partially starved (l-g15 50/51 dead, al-g7 1/50, l-g28 5/46), so fix the budget finding first, then re-measure true edge-extractor recall — the r13-r15 windows show the residual gap is real but smaller (13/30 audits). Coverage gaps of 30-60% zero-edge records persist even in healthy r15 namespaces.

extraction_findings.name: Itemized-list enumeration (per-item claims, item-to-subject binding, ordinal rank)
extraction_findings.recommendation: Ranked above higher-count families because list enumeration directly starves count and membership questions ('how many X', 'list the X', 'which was second') — the known miss taxonomy's highest-value class alongside dates, and already a proven cost family (itemized-list P1b). At write time, detect enumeration structures (bullets, bold headers, comma lists, numbered steps, price/deal rows, survey/spec tables) and distribute into per-item claims that preserve three things the current extractor loses: parent-list binding, each item's own attributes bound to the item rather than the list head (l-g10 price binding, al-g4 year/date attributes), and ordinal rank as a typed field. Also guard against silent mid-list truncation (al-g2, l-g22 partial drops).

extraction_findings.name: Speaker/subject attribution (first-person, pronoun/fragment, junk-subject claims; user-vs-assistant confusion)
extraction_findings.recommendation: Add canonical-subject resolution before any claim is stored: map first-person/elliptical/adverb-initial utterances ('Just moved...', 'Keep forgetting...') and pronoun/fragment subjects to the speaker's canonical entity, resolve second-person facts to the addressee, tag every claim with speaker role (user vs assistant vs third party — l-g17/l-g26 store assistant advice as user facts), and reject-or-repair junk subjects ('Here','And','The','Correct') by requiring the subject to resolve to a known entity or the speaker. This is a force-multiplier: facts in these audits were usually EXTRACTED but are invisible to typed/subject-keyed lookup, so fixing attribution recovers already-captured claims across every other family.

extraction_findings.name: Negation / retraction / supersession (polarity dropped or inverted; invalid_at never used; state-change not linked)
extraction_findings.recommendation: Introduce a first-class negative/retraction claim type: extract polarity explicitly (several audits show negated preferences stored INVERTED as positives — worse than a miss because it feeds confidently wrong answers), emit typed negation/cessation claims for denials, ended states, 'used to' transitions, and disposal events, and link each retraction to its target via a supersession edge that sets invalid_at on the superseded claim (currently never used). Include a scope guard so supersession only deactivates the matched claim, not unrelated edges (the mo-g3@r14 wrongful-deactivation case), and let corrections retire the corrected assertion instead of coexisting as flat active claims (l-g25).

extraction_findings.name: Future plans / scheduled events (appointments, prospective commitments, proposal-to-final chains)
extraction_findings.recommendation: Type prospective commitments as future-dated event claims (planned=true, scheduled_time, participants, duration) instead of leaving them untyped, and model negotiation chains — proposal, retraction, final agreement — so the agreed plan supersedes earlier proposals (mo-g1@r14's proposal-retraction-final case). Falls out largely for free once the relative-date resolver (finding 1) and supersession linking (finding 7) exist; conversational (mo-*) windows only, so it is dialogue-plan specific.

extraction_findings.name: Photo/media-share attribution (image-grounded facts credited to session id or pseudo-entity, not the sharer)
extraction_findings.recommendation: When a turn contains a media share, create a share-event with the speaker as agent and attribute all caption- and image-derived facts to the sharer's canonical entity rather than the session id or a session pseudo-entity (mo-g4@r14). A narrow, well-bounded fix — a share-detection hook plus reuse of the canonical-subject resolver from finding 6 — that closes a persistent multimodal-attribution hole appearing in all three conversational windows.

extraction_findings.name: Embedded structured payloads (JSON blobs, numeric data tables) sentence-shredded with near-zero tail extraction
extraction_findings.recommendation: Detect structured payloads (JSON bodies, embedded tables, spec rows) before sentence-splitting and route them through a structure-aware extractor that emits key-path or row-wise claims; the current sentence shredder produces near-zero usable claims from these regions. Low occurrence count, so lowest priority — but cheap to detect (brace/pipe density heuristics) and it removes a class of records where extraction is effectively blind.

judge_findings.name: answer_correct_judge_wrong (confirmed judge false-negatives)
member_refs: r13/c7_q41 (multi-hop): full nb_answer states all gold items candles+essential oils+music; prelim_contains_gold=true; judge scored false
r14/c4_q7 (multi-hop): answer names all 3 gold locations (London, California, Smoky Mountains) with correct Tim attribution; superset extras all dataset-true
r14/c7_q44 (temporal): first sentence states exact gold date April 9, 2023; trailing Anna hedge is source-faithful; prelim_contains_gold=true
r14/c2_q32 (multi-hop): answer states both gold facts (hiking, mountaineering) verbatim as first two bullets; superset (yoga) dataset-supported
r15/c9_q64 run0 (multi-hop): answer states all 4 gold activities; judge dinged grounded superset (band/logo bullets); pinned-judge replay reproduces False 3/3 on full answer, True on gold-only answer
r15/c9_q64 run1 (multi-hop): same sample, independent run, same judge false-negative on an answer containing every gold fact
r15/c3_q115 (single-hop): gold stated by paraphrase+superset ('dramas' verbatim; 'emotionally-driven' via near-synonymous D1:14 self-description; citation [2] contains gold verbatim)
lme_s_r1/afdc33df (single-session-preference): all 4 rubric elements satisfied with 10/10 grounded quotes; only blemish is the hedge 'If you have granite countertops'
judge_findings.recommendation: 7 unique samples (c9_q64 confirmed twice across runs). These are the rows the deterministic pre-check (finding 5) targets; until it ships, treat single-judge correct=false on superset/paraphrase answers as unreliable and require the pinned-judge replay protocol used here (temp 0.0, 3x) before citing them as model failures.

judge_findings.name: gold_label_defective — CONFIRMED dataset defects for quarantine list
member_refs: QUARANTINE locomo10.json conv-4 qa[86] (c4_q86, r13): speaker misattribution — question asks what JOHN shared on his skype call, but sole evidence D5:1 is TIM's turn ('Just skyped with that Harry Potter fan... We talked characters'); no other skype mention in the conversation; unanswerable as posed for John. Store record mem_c1ddf2dd13de4303 confirms 'Tim:' prefix.

QUARANTINE locomo10.json conv-44 qa[81] (c5_q81, r14): temporal-anchor generation artifact — template instantiated with the resolved EVENT date instead of session date: 'the Monday before July 24, 2023' literally denotes July 17 (July 24 IS a Monday); the pet-shelter event happened ON July 24 per its own evidence D13:1 (session dated July 27). Gold is false for the question as worded; model resolved both dates correctly.

QUARANTINE locomo10.json conv-47 qa[64] (c6_q64, r14): annotation arithmetic error — gold 'nearly four months' contradicts its own evidence turns: chess start 22 Jul 2022 (D17:1) to win 1 Nov 2022 (D30:2/D30:4, 'this Tuesday' before Sat Nov 5) = 102-106 days = 3.35-3.48 months; model's 'approximately three and a half months' is the correct computation.

QUARANTINE locomo10.json conv-50 qa[137] (c9_q137, r15, adjudicated 3x independently — all concur): speaker misattribution — gold 'at an early age' for Calvin derives from D26:6, DAVE's autobiographical origin story ('My dad took me to my first car show when I was 10') answering Calvin's question to Dave (D26:5); exhaustive scan shows Calvin never dates his own car interest anywhere; model's abstention is the only faithful answer.

QUARANTINE locomo10.json conv-48 qa[116] (c7_q116, r15, adjudicated 2x — concur): speaker misattribution — question subject is Jolene but sole evidence D7:20 ('Exercise is key for me - it makes me feel connected to my body') is DEBORAH's line; Jolene never says the gold phrase; model correctly reported Jolene's actual statements (calmer/clearer/grounded).
(dup of c9_q137 — second independent adjudication, same defect)
(dup of c9_q137 — third independent adjudication, same defect)
(dup of c7_q116 — second independent adjudication, same defect)
judge_findings.recommendation: Quarantine 5 unique question IDs from /Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json: conv4-qa86, conv44-qa81, conv47-qa64, conv50-qa137, conv48-qa116. Defect taxonomy: 3 speaker misattributions, 1 temporal-anchor template bug, 1 annotator arithmetic error. Exclude from all accuracy denominators in every rotation window (they recur across r13/r14/r15 since the rotation reuses conversations); the multiply-adjudicated rows (c9_q137 3/3, c7_q116 2/2 concurrence) are the highest-confidence entries. Apply quarantine BEFORE any judge pre-check so defective-gold rows never reach the auto-pass logic.

judge_findings.name: answer_defensible_gold_ambiguous (judge applied gold faithfully — NOT judge errors, NOT quarantinable)
member_refs: r14/c4_q113: gold 'The Wolves' for Tim is annotator implicature (Tim only said 'solid'; the 'my team' assertion is John's, and John is a Wolves PLAYER); model's abstention-with-correct-attribution equally defensible
r14/c9_q113: question word 'activity' better matches model's 'pondered the intricacies of life' than gold's sensation 'wind blowing through his hair' — both from the same evidence sentence D20:4

r14/c9_q7: gold 'Yes' to 'a lot of people?' rests solely on an image caption of an unidentified group (stock-photo query was 'grand opening'); model cited the exact evidence turn and abstained on the quantifier
judge_findings.recommendation: Leave these scored as-is (the judge correctly enforced the gold), but tag them 'ambiguous-gold' in the scoreboard and exclude them from judge-error-rate metrics. They are the systematic cost of verify-or-abstain behavior against inference-heavy golds — the same failure signature as the 2026-07-09 numeric-aggregation headline: faithful abstention scored as wrong.

judge_findings.name: model_actually_wrong — fleet-1 judge-questionable claims that did NOT survive
judge_findings.recommendation: Honest deflation of fleet 1's 19-row cluster: ZERO rows collapsed to model_actually_wrong — every disputed row survived as not-the-model's-fault. But the 'judge is broken' framing still deflates sharply: only 8/19 rows (7 unique samples) are true judge false-negatives; 8 rows (5 unique) shift blame to the DATASET (the judge faithfully enforced a defective gold — judge blameless); 3 rows are washes on ambiguous golds (judge blameless). Net: fleet 1 was 100% right that the model wasn't wrong, but only ~42% of rows implicate the judge itself. Correct headline: judged-accuracy on these windows understates true accuracy by up to 15 unique samples, split roughly evenly between judge miscalls and LoCoMo label defects.

judge_findings.name: RECOMMENDATION: deterministic gold-containment override (pre-judge short-circuit)
member_refs: flips r14/c7_q44 (ISO date canon: '9 April, 2023' == 'April 9, 2023'; gold match precedes hedge)
flips r15/c3_q115 (atom 'dramas' 1/1; atom 'emotionally-driven films' 2/3 lemmas: emotionally+films)
judge_findings.recommendation: PRECISE RULE — insert in bench/notebooklm_freetier_report.py between judge_rows assembly and the judge_locomo call, operating on the FULL untruncated nb_answer (never the display-truncated given_answer), and ONLY after quarantine filtering: (1) NORMALIZE gold G and answer A: lowercase; strip markdown emphasis/citation brackets; fold hyphens to spaces; collapse whitespace; canonicalize all date expressions to ISO-8601; light stemming; drop stopwords. (2) ELIGIBILITY: skip to LLM judge if G's content-lemma set is empty or is only {yes,no,none,not} — bare yes/no golds are never auto-passed (protects c9_q7). (3) ATOMIZE: split G on top-level commas, semicolons, and ' and '. (4) MATCH: an atom passes iff >=2/3 of its content lemmas (for date atoms, the exact ISO date) occur in normalized A. (5) DENIAL GUARD: find the earliest offset of any atom match in A; if regex (no|not|never|do(es)? not) within 6 tokens of (mention|specif|state|provide|record|information|timeframe) matches at an EARLIER offset, do not auto-pass (protects c4_q113, whose 'no specific mention' headline precedes its Wolves reference, while still passing c7_q44, whose gold date precedes its hedge). (6) If all atoms pass and the guard is clear, set correct=true deterministically and skip the LLM judge; the pre-check can only flip false->true, never true->false, so no correctly-scored-true row is ever touched. VERIFIED AGAINST ALL 19 ROWS: flips exactly the 5 listed ACJW rows; touches zero correctly-scored rows (c4_q113 blocked by guard, c9_q7 by eligibility, c9_q113/c7_q116/c9_q137/c6_q64 by atom failure, defective-gold rows removed upstream by quarantine). HONEST RESIDUAL: 3 of the 8 ACJW rows are NOT flippable by any safe string rule — r15/c9_q64 both runs ('weekly visits to local parks' vs 'regular walks with friends in the park' is 1/4 lemma overlap; loosening the threshold to catch it would risk flipping c9_q113-style rows) and lme_s_r1/afdc33df (rubric gold with no extractive atoms); those two samples still need the LLM judge, mitigated by logging the pre-check's per-atom scores as judge context and by the 3x-replay protocol.

verification.reason: Verified against live stores in two windows (lme_s_r1_codex and holdout_rotation_r13_codex, corroborated by r14/r15). Claims are 100% session-stamped with zero backdating: claims.valid_at == source memories.valid_at for 1606/1606 (r13 locomo-g0-r0), 5245/5245 (lme longmemeval-g0-r0), 5135/5135 (lme longmemeval-g11-r0), 18972/18972 (r14 fleet), 20037/20037 (r15 fleet). Relative dates survive only as literal unresolved claim text (e.g. claim_1d567f8049: predicate 'buy', object 'last week'; claim_9fbb9fe4: interval claim with object 'last month'; 'gym'/'yesterday') while valid_at carries the session timestamp; the anchors field is empty on 100% of claims and no claim json contains any resolved-date key. Edges likewise inherit session time (141/141 in r13 g0; engine.py:1600 passes valid_at=rec.valid_at). The only resolver output channel is the events table (normalize_dates at engine.py:1588): the two lme member namespaces have ZERO events, and in r13 g0 133/141 events default to the session-day window while the 8 deviating ones are misresolutions (guitar model-year '1968' smeared onto unrelated facts including a 'last Friday' photo; 'next month' resolved to the current month) — no genuine backdating anywhere. One caveat: the recommendation should say 'wire the existing eidetic/events.py normalize_dates resolver into claim/edge valid_at' rather than 'add' one, since a deterministic write-time resolver already exists but never touches claims or edges; the gap itself is real.

Re-checked live stores in two randomly selected windows (holdout_rotation_r13_codex, holdout_rotation_r15_codex). The finding's core assertion — dated events stamped with session time, NEVER backdated — is falsified: event-type claims carry a filters.event_date field with deterministically resolved event times (207 claims in r13, 212 in r15; 89 per window genuinely backdated vs session date), and sampled resolutions are correct for exactly the claimed-missing fact class ('one year ago' → 2022-10-08 vs session 2023-10-08; 'got married last week' → 2023-12-19 vs session 2023-12-27; 'graduated last week' → 2023-03-26 vs session 2023-04-02). The recommended fix (add a deterministic write-time date resolver with dual timestamping) already exists in eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py (_event_date_claims_from_atom/_segment_event_date, wired at line 1224) and record_ops.py (_relative_date_from_atom), including date_precision granularity and ambient-year guards. What IS true: the valid_at column itself always equals session time (0/18,927 r13 and 0/20,037 r15 claims deviate) and event_date coverage is thin (~11-13% of event claims), so a narrower coverage/utilization finding could be re-filed — but the claim as stated ('never backdated', resolver absent) does not hold against the live store.

Re-checked two members from different windows directly against the live stores. (1) mo-g2-r0@r13_codex (namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g2-r0, artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data/eidetic.sqlite): all 2,113 claims and all 178 edges have valid_at identical to the source memory's session timestamp (0 backdated); 163/168 events carry session-day windows, and the 5 exceptions are misresolutions, not real backdating — all five inherit one July-2023 window from mem_020217ea5b354459 whose text never mentions July, while 'going to Tokyo next month' (correct: Nov 2023, sitting unused in metadata.dates) and 'started August last year' (correct: Aug 2022) get no resolved event time in any claim/edge/event. 66 claims have relative-date proof atoms ('recently', 'next month'), all session-stamped. (2) l-g11-r0@_r1_codex (eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g11-r0, artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data/eidetic.sqlite): all 5,135 claims session-stamped, and the namespace has 0 edges and 0 events, so nothing carries event time; concrete gap-class facts confirmed ('networking breakfast last month' event-claim stamped at session 2023-05-24; 'sister's birthday three weeks ago' stamped 2023-05-26). Derived table is gists only; no other temporal structure. Code confirms the mechanism: eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py:200 writes claims with valid_at=rec.valid_at unconditionally and engine.py:1601 does the same for edges, while ingest-parsed dates (metadata.dates, present on 22/30 and 29/46 memories) are never promoted to claim/edge event time — exactly the dual-timestamp gap the finding describes and its recommendation targets. Only caveat: 'never backdated' is not literally absolute for the events table (a write-time normalize_dates resolver exists), but in both re-checked members it produced either session-day windows or wrong windows, so the substantive claim — the missed fact class lacks correctly resolved event-time claims/edges — holds.

All three re-checks confirm. (1) r13/c7_q41: dataset gold (conv-48 qa[41]) = "candles, music, essential oils"; full nb_answer contains all three ("candles and essential oils" verbatim, dedicated Music section); prelim_contains_gold=true; judged file correct=false — judge false-negative verified. (2) r14/c7_q44: dataset gold (conv-48 qa[44]) = "on 9 April, 2023"; answer's first sentence states "April 9, 2023"; session_12 dated 9 April 2023; hedge is source-faithful (D12:1 says art show "with a friend" while Anna is only ever "my neighbor" per D3:4); prelim_contains_gold=True; judged correct=False — judge false-negative verified. (3) c4_q86: dataset index 4 (sample_id conv-43) qa[86] asks what JOHN shared on his skype call, but sole evidence D5:1 is verbatim TIM's turn ("Just skyped with that Harry Potter fan... We talked characters"); exhaustive scan finds no other skype mention in the conversation; store record mem_c1ddf2dd13de4303 in r13 eidetic.sqlite confirms the "Tim:" prefix (source c4_session_5) — speaker-misattribution gold defect verified. Minor nit only: the quarantine list writes "conv-4" for this row but the actual sample_id is conv-43 (index 4); fix the label before applying the quarantine, though the qa index and defect are correct.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: dated-event time resolution (relative dates never resolved into claim valid_at)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: My partner and I told our extended fam about our marriage yesterday – it was so special!
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The event claim exists ([event] Evan | told extended family about marriage) but its valid_at=1704576720.0 equals the record's session timestamp — 0 of 1606 claims in this namespace carry a valid_at different from their source record. 'Got married last week', 'check-up Monday', 'started diet last Monday', 'drove to Banff last month', 'honeymoon next month' are all pinned to conversation time. Bi-temporal as_of recall and LoCoMo when-questions are off by days-to-months; interval claims even store the offset (val=2 unit=years) but never apply it.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: By the way, I've started a new diet—limiting myself to just two ginger snaps a day.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Captured only as a state claim whose value is the full sentence with unit empty (an edge 'Evan -[restricting diet to]-> two ginger snaps per day' exists, but no quantity claim). Namespace-wide: exactly 1 claim_type='quantity' and only 4/1606 claims with any numeric value+unit. Untyped numbers mean the deterministic SMQE/structured_recall path cannot compare or aggregate — the same failure family as the known measure_sum over-sum leak (#41/#42).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Sam: No, I didn't make it.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: (Sam denying he baked the pie — it was from his cousin's wedding, mem_9ad4bcc1.) The denial survives only as a verbatim shingle state with junk predicate 'sam no i didn'; claim types present are state/event/preference/interval/quantity — no negation/retraction type anywhere. Same for 'Nah, it's just my homemade sauce' (denies family-secret) and 'Nah, no changes for me'. False-premise/adversarial questions need a first-class negative for verify-or-abstain; predicate-level structured queries cannot see denials and latest-assertion-wins retraction machinery never engages.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: [shares a photo: a photo of a bowl of spinach, avocado, and strawberries]
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Photo-share claims get subject = session id ('c8_session_8' / 'c8_session_23') instead of the speaker (Sam shared this photo of his diet food, mem_e0f95f0e). LoCoMo asks many photo-grounded questions ('what did X share a photo of?'); entity-scoped retrieval or graph lookup keyed on Sam/Evan misses the visual evidence entirely.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g0-r0 (artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex): 25 records, 1606 claims (state 1365, event 120, preference 109, interval 11, quantity 1), 181 edges. Extraction fires on every record (42-102 claims, 3-20 non-inferred edges each) — nothing dead. Architecture is two-layer: a curated semantic layer (typed claims like 'Evan | told extended family about marriage', edges like 'Evan -[owns]-> 1968 Kustom K-200A vintage guitar', 'Evan -[married]-> partner') plus a per-sentence verbatim shingle layer (subject = first token, value = full sentence). Keyword probes confirm all high-value facts are present in some form: guitar model, Prius breakdown, marriage/pregnancy, Banff trip, Canada honeymoon, medical check-up, Weight Watchers. Verdict 'patchy' NOT for missing facts but for whole typed classes missing from the claim layer: event-date resolution 0/1606, typed quantities 4/1606, typed negations 0 — these break the deterministic structured_recall/as_of paths even though NLI over verbatim sentence values can still recall the facts. Secondary noise (not a missed class): shingle claims with junk subjects ('It's', 'Take', 'Here's', 'Don't', session ids) and misfired preference typing ('Focusing | like | a plan' from 'sounds like a plan'; 'Evan | like | that' from 'things like that'). Also ~40 edges have empty source_memory_id (incl. co_activated), so edge-to-record provenance is partially broken. Sampled in depth: mem_f08a2107b4054ce5 (shortest, 1918 chars, 50 claims), mem_e0f95f0e2d50436d (longest, 4487, 92 claims), mem_9ad4bcc10dab459f (4248, 102 claims); plus corpus-wide scans (numeric sentences 34, negation sentences 26) and keyword probes across ~8 more records.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: derived numeric quantities (ages, counts) never typed as quantity claims
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: A friend made it for my 18th birthday ten years ago. (mem_76e5e2eb1eb74058)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only 3 quantity claims exist in 1521. The age fact (Caroline ~28) survives only as opaque state-claim text ('has hand-painted bowl made by | A friend made it for my 18th birthday ten years ago'), so the deterministic path (structured_recall/value_as_of/measure queries) cannot answer 'how old is Caroline' without free-text arithmetic — exactly the numeric-aggregation surface where verify-or-abstain is already known to leak (5/13 verified-WRONG headline).

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: conflicting entity counts co-exist with no reconciliation or quantity typing
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: edge: 'Melanie has 2 younger kids' (mem_76e5e2eb1eb74058) vs edge: 'Melanie has three children (inferred from photo description ...)' (mem_5c232325bcb24015)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: A 'how many children does Melanie have?' question hits two contradictory graph facts, one photo-inferred, neither superseding the other and neither typed as quantity — a verifier will happily entail either count.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: partial negations / lost-relationship retractions get no typed claim
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Some close friends kept supporting me, but a few weren't able to handle it. (mem_1b79d77f51644448)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The lost-friendships fact exists only as a whole-sentence untyped state claim; there is no typed negation/retraction, so false-premise defense and 'did anyone stop supporting Caroline?' cannot be answered on the deterministic typed path (31/1521 claims carry any negation token, all as raw sentence text).

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: future intentions/plans mostly untyped (no future-dated event claims)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: We could do a family outting, or wanna plan something special for this summer, just us two? (mem_d63a187d92604ed0); 'I'm feeling inspired by autumn so I'm planning a few' (mem_8927646bf03e4bfc)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Zero typed claims match 'planning'; forward-intent facts survive only as untyped sentence claims, so LoCoMo-style 'what does X plan to do?' questions depend entirely on free-read over raw text. Partially covered (e.g. 'Caroline is organizing LGBTQ art show next month' did get typed event + edge) but the class is patchy.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g1-r0 (artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex): 19 records, 1521 claims, 160 edges — verified live. Every record has extraction: 41-129 claims each (roughly per-sentence blanket of 970 untyped whole-sentence claims plus 551 typed), 16/19 records have edges. Strong coverage confirmed by spot-checks: all 141 event claims carry valid_at with relative dates resolved (e.g. 'yesterday'→2023-05-07, 'last week'→2023-08-18); itemized lists decomposed per-item (adoption docs → separate claims for references/financial info/medical checks); relationships rich (is_parent_of, 'has been married for 5 years', necklace-from-grandmother-in-Sweden, Matt Patterson performed at daughter's birthday); 137 preference claims; 13 interval claims covering durations ('started transitioning three years ago', 'goes to the beach once or twice a year'); 116 photo captions all preserved with ~130 photo-referencing claims. Health = healthy: the misses are TYPED-claim classes (quantity typing is nearly absent at 3/1521), not raw coverage — the untyped sentence blanket means the free-read path sees everything, but the deterministic/verified path is blind to numbers. Secondary quality note (not coverage): typed-claim subject/predicate extraction is noisy — junk subjects like 'What', 'Some', 'Got', \"Can't\", and one leaked session id 'c0_session_4' as claim subject; values still hold full source sentences so recall degrades gracefully. Inspection command as given in the task, DATA_DIR=artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Calvin: I only work in the studio. I have another room for relaxation with a TV
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 27 negation cues ('only', 'haven't', 'never', "don't") across the 30 records produced ZERO negation/retraction-typed claims (claim-type histogram: state 1782, event 173, preference 140, interval 18, negation 0). Without negation claims, verify-or-abstain cannot answer false-premise questions ('Does Calvin watch TV in his studio?') with a cited 'No — <premise>'; the store only holds positives, so adversarial LoCoMo questions either abstain or verify against the wrong positive.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: yesterday I came back from San Francsico with some great insights and knowledge on car modification
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The only claim covering this sentence is a garbage interval ([interval] Dave | 'i m tied up' | 'San Francsico with...'); there is no event claim 'Dave returned from San Francisco' with a resolved valid_at. Store-wide, only 11 of 22 relative temporal qualifiers (yesterday/last week/etc.) surface in any claim pred/obj. Temporal recall ('when did Dave return from SF') and value_as_of/fact_history time travel need resolved dated events, not blob states.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Hey Cal, been ages since we spoke!
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Dave addressing Calvin as 'Cal' was extracted 13 times as claim [Dave | nickname | Cal] — the nickname is attributed to the SPEAKER, not the addressee. The correct fact (Calvin's nickname is Cal) is absent. Inverted alias claims will merge/attribute facts to the wrong person at recall time, and NLI verification passes them because the proof atom literally contains 'Cal' — same verified-WRONG failure mode as the 2026-07-09 numeric-aggregation headline.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: [shares a photo: a photo of a book with a boarding pass and a boarding pass]
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Photo-share claims get subject = session id ('c9_session_17', 'c9_session_28') instead of the sharer (Calvin shared the boarding-pass photo right after 'I booked a flight ticket to Boston'). Only 6 'shares photo of' edges exist versus dozens of photo shares in the raw text. LoCoMo image-grounded questions ('what did Calvin share a photo of?') cannot be retrieved by person-subject index.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g2-r0 (30 recs / 2113 claims / 178 edges) at artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data. Sampled 8 records (shortest mem_60b33272 1510B/37 claims; longest mem_25acd723 6604B/132 claims/17 edges; plus mem_a6020e19, mem_84b7c364, mem_909efd15, mem_faee4f25, mem_00c0ccc7, mem_b7528c3f). Extraction runs on every record — not dead — and the typed layer has real wins: events (Calvin threw album party w/ valid_at, Calvin toured/met/collaborated-with Frank Ocean edges), preferences, ownership edges. But 1782/2113 (84%) claims are state-type raw sentence copies with first-word predicates, and 640 of those have non-person junk subjects (Been/It's/Goodbye/Take/Keep) — ~30% of all claims are unindexable by person and dilute subject-keyed recall. Quantities are a non-issue in this window (only number in corpus is '10', and it appears in claims). Most consequential systemic defects: zero negation claims (breaks false-premise refutation), inverted alias claims (verified-wrong risk), session-id photo subjects, and ~50% loss of relative-date anchors outside blob states.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I noticed they weren't acting normally, so I made an appointment with an animal behaviorist last Wed. (mem_f758b729709340a9)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The event claim 'made appointment with animal behaviorist' gets valid_at = the session date (Sat 2023-10-28), not the referenced Wednesday; same for 'We had a doggy playdate last Friday' (anchored to Mon 2023-07-03). Every 'last Wed/Friday/Saturday' event in the namespace is off by days, so LOCOMO temporal questions and value_as_of/fact_history time-travel answer with the wrong event time.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Andrew: No, no pets right now. But I do love animals. (mem_44626d3de2cb4dee)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only captured as a span-fallback claim with junk predicate 'andrew no no pets'; there is no typed (Andrew, has pets, none) claim. structured_recall/value_as_of on Andrew's pets returns nothing instead of a citable 'No', and when Andrew later adopts Scout (mem_1e071bb5, 2023-11-22) there is no negation claim to supersede — the verify-or-abstain 'No — <premise>' path has nothing to fire on.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Two of them are Jack Russell mixes and the other two are Chihuahua mixes. (mem_4e57f4ba6e4947af, 2023-07-03) vs 'Pepper and Panda are Lab mixes, and Precious and Pixie are Chihuahua mixes.' (mem_f758b729709340a9, 2023-10-28)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 of 2146 claims have invalid_at set — both breed versions (and both their edges) are simultaneously active, so verification can cite either era's fact as current truth; latest-assertion-wins never engaged in this namespace even where the corpus directly contradicts itself.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: It's a tasty dish that's easy to make and loaded with healthy stuff like chicken, garlic, lemon, and herbs. (mem_4e57f4ba6e4947af)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Ingredients only survive inside one junk span claim ('It's | like | make and loaded with healthy stuff...'); no per-item claims/edges (Roasted Chicken → contains → lemon), so enumeration questions ('what's in Audrey's roasted chicken?') can't be answered by the deterministic typed path — contrast with dog names, which DID get per-item edges (Audrey has dog Pepper/Precious/Panda).

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g3-r0 (28 recs / 2146 claims / 232 edges): extraction is HEALTHY and dense — no dead records (min 43, max 126 claims/record, monotone with record length), and it is two-layered: (1) a semantic layer with real predicates (state 1757 / preference 214 / event 151 / interval 15 / quantity 9) that captures the headline facts — job 'Financial Analyst', dog names Pepper/Precious/Panda/Pixie as individual edges, 'four dogs' quantity captured 3x, '3 years', 'two hours' hike, favorite recipe Chicken Pot Pie, go-to ingredient garlic, Fox Hollow trail, girlfriend edge, Scout adoption; (2) an exhaustive sentence-span fallback that covers essentially every utterance (so NLI/free-read recall can still ground anything). The misses are systematic, not random: (a) event claims inherit the session timestamp — relative dates ('last Wed', 'last Friday', 'last Saturday') are never resolved, so all dated-event claims carry wrong valid_at by 1-6 days; (b) negations exist only as junk-predicate span claims, never typed, so the structured path can't answer absence questions or trigger supersession; (c) supersession machinery never fired: 0/2146 claims have invalid_at even for the direct Jack-Russell-vs-Lab breed contradiction between sessions; (d) span-layer noise: ~40% of claims have junk subjects ('It's', 'Can't', 'Talk', 'c5_session_X') and first-token predicates, including speaker mis-attribution (Audrey as subject of 'Andrew: Sure.'), which dilutes typed-claim precision though it does not reduce coverage. Sampled records: mem_3eacf90753a24f98, mem_f758b729709340a9, mem_34ebcd28762949d8, mem_4e57f4ba6e4947af, mem_44626d3de2cb4dee, mem_a961aa8ccf224e73, mem_79b5167e0bc2411d, mem_bc0d8713fd8646e7.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: dated events — relative-date resolution (event time vs mention time)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Crazy thing happened - I was in the final of a big Valorant tournament last Saturday, and I won! (mem_150b1edc94004037; the claim exists but valid_at=1667880600, identical to the session timestamp — audited all 1928 claims: 1928/1928 have valid_at == record valid_at, 0 resolved 'last Saturday/last Friday/last week' to the actual event date)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Bi-temporal recall (as_of, value_as_of, fact_history) and LoCoMo 'when did X happen' questions date every event to the conversation day, up to a week late; supersession ordering can invert for events discussed out of order.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: negative-outcome events lose speaker attribution (subject = sentence fragment)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Tough tournament, didn't make it to the finals. (mem_f2dddeec65974f33; only claim is [state] subj='Tough' pred='tough tournament didn t' — the loss is never attributed to Nate. 217/1928 claims (~11%) have junk subjects like It's/Keep/Your/Must/Check)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Person-scoped structured recall never sees the loss while wins ARE attributed (edge Nate--won->Valorant tournament), so 'how many tournaments did Nate win/lose' over-counts wins — feeds directly into the open numeric-aggregation verify-or-abstain leak (#41/#42).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: It's gonna be my 4th one! (mem_1db246c603e048b0; stored only as verbatim [state], no quantity claim; likewise 'The tank is big enough now for three' in mem_f2dddeec. Only 7 quantity claims and 8 non-empty units exist across 1928 claims)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The deterministic measure/sum path in structured_recall cannot aggregate untyped numbers embedded in prose; counts must fall back to generative reading, which is where the 5/13 verified-WRONG numeric answers came from.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: My brother wrote it - he used to make me these cute notes when we were kids. (mem_150b1edc94004037; only capture is a [state] claim subj='Joanna' pred='were kids'; grep of all 227 edges: 0 person-to-person edges, no Joanna--has_brother edge)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Multi-hop relationship questions ('who wrote the letter Joanna put in her script?') cannot traverse the graph; the fact is only reachable via fuzzy text match on a claim whose predicate ('were kids') doesn't mention the brother.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g4-r0 (29 recs / 1928 claims / 227 edges). Records are LoCoMo dialogue sessions between Nate and Joanna (1.3k-6k chars). Sampled 6 records (shortest mem_3dbf6f2cb6654f34, longest mem_150b1edc94004037/mem_f2dddeec65974f33, plus mem_72a195e4, mem_6f6a912d, mem_c76805da) plus corpus-wide probes. Extraction style: near-total sentence shredding — every sentence lands as a verbatim [state] claim (claim_type: state 1542, preference 202, event 168, interval 9, quantity 7), so NLI/free-read recall has raw material for almost everything. Normalization is where quality lives: strong for salient entities (Nate--has pet->Max, Max--is type of->dog, Joanna--is allergic to->turtles/cockroaches/reptiles, Joanna is lactose intolerant=true, Tilly disambiguated as stuffed-animal-now vs had-dog-before, gaming party 7 attendees/6 returning, Nate participated in 3 tournaments, rated 9 out of 10). 7/29 records have 0 edges (mem_c54b0f70, mem_d57cde0d, mem_a9d674f1, mem_ad043570, mem_60ccb8df, mem_3dbf6f2c, mem_af277028) but all still have 38-72 claims, and their entities often get edges from other records. Not patchy/dead: substantive facts are recallable; the 4 listed classes are systematic normalization gaps (event-time resolution 0%, typed numerics ~0.4%, person-person edges 0, ~11% claims with fragment subjects) that specifically hurt deterministic/temporal/aggregation paths rather than verbatim retrieval. Repro script: /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-raunakgengiti-Eidetic-Plus/eb09f70d-9fc9-40be-884b-dc6507a26ca8/scratchpad/dump.py (run with DATA_DIR=artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data). Gotcha hit during audit: record text lives in .text not .content, and a stray inspect.py in the scratchpad shadowed stdlib inspect breaking eidetic imports (removed).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: And I also don’t like bowling itself, to be honest.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Stored as [preference] John | like | bowling itself (mem_7dbfa974f7254ebc) — the structured triple asserts the OPPOSITE of the source. Same record also inverts "I can’t share my love for him". Preference-profile / structured_recall on predicate=like returns a wrong fact, and since proof_atom carries the full sentence the claim looks source-backed. Negation-as-retraction is a headline store feature; this class is worse than missing — it is wrong. (Counter-examples exist: "John has not gotten a dog" WAS captured, so handling is inconsistent, not absent.)

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: relative-date events not backdated (valid_at = session time)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: By the way, three days ago I bought myself an adventure book with fantasy novels and cool arts.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Claim [event] James bought adventure book has valid_at=2022-04-29 = the session date, not 2022-04-26. Same for "I played one with friends two days ago" and "yesterday I went bowling" (claim valid_at=2022-03-17; one duplicate claim resolved 2022-03-16 but only into the value field). LOCOMO temporal questions ("when did X happen") and as_of time-travel answer off by 1-3 days for essentially every relatively-dated event.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: That's my sister and my dogs. We were just chilling together yesterday
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: mem_2eab04d27e284b11 (4615 chars) produced ZERO edges and its only claim for this is degenerate ([state] James | james that s my). Same record drops "For both you and your Jill!" entirely (who Jill is to John is never claimed anywhere). "James has a sister" / "John knows Jill" are unanswerable via the entity-relation/edge path — relationship questions are a core LOCOMO category.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: entity-anchored locations and named items mentioned obliquely
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: In addition, I even managed to get out to another country. The city of Nuuk, if you know.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only extraction is [state] The | the city of nuuk — subject "The", no James→visited→Nuuk claim or edge (record has 0 edges). Same failure mode in mem_0ce30c4e548f4d15: John's book recommendation "Heard of \"The Name of the Wind\"?" becomes subject="Heard". Travel-destination and recommendation questions are LOCOMO staples; entity-keyed claim/graph lookup misses these because the subject anchor is a stopword.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g5-r0 @ artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data: 31 records / 2199 claims / 360 edges confirmed. Sampled 8 records (4 longest, 2 shortest, 2 mid). Volume is dense — every record has 40-109 claims — but two-tier: ~1409/2199 (64%) are degenerate sentence-splat with garbled predicates (\"james hey john\", \"shares a photo a\") and ~330 have junk subjects (It/The/They/Can't...). The substantive tier (~790 claims) covers most core facts well: dog names Max/Daisy, \"James has three dogs\", Luna = John's cousin's dog, CS:GO tournament→dog shelter→homeless chain, Civilization VI interval \"a month\", some absolute-date resolution (bowling 2022-03-16, programming group 2022-08-19 — but into value, not valid_at). Edge coverage is uneven: 2/31 records (mem_0ce30c4e548f4d15 session_8, mem_2eab04d27e284b11 session_17) got 0 edges despite containing relationship/location/recommendation facts, and edge extraction shows systematic duplicates (same edge emitted twice). Verdict patchy, not dead: no record is empty, but whole classes fail systematically — negation polarity inverted in preference triples, relative dates never backdated into claim valid_at, third-party relationships and stopword-subject facts (Nuuk, The Name of the Wind, sister, Jill) invisible to entity-keyed recall. Dump of sampled records+claims+edges: /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-raunakgengiti-Eidetic-Plus/eb09f70d-9fc9-40be-884b-dc6507a26ca8/scratchpad/sample_dump.txt

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: negations / retractions (ended states never become typed negative claims; invalid_at never used)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: He was such an important part of our family for 10 years and it's so hard to think he's not here wagging that tail anymore.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Max's death (mem_3ee31cd1) yields only positive claims/edges ('Max was part of John's family', 'Max is a dog') with no ended-state or death event, and 0 of 2,165 claims have invalid_at set. Similarly 'Maria: No, I didn't. [have pets growing up]' (mem_3c344984) survives only as the junk verbatim state 'Maria | maria no i didn'. Supersession, false-premise defense, and 'does John still have a dog?' queries all fail because the store's typed layer asserts only the positive era.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: relative-date resolution for dated events (all claims stamped with session time, never event time)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I graduated last week!
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The event claim 'John | graduated | university degree' (mem_a770e4f4) carries valid_at=1680453360 (session date 2023-04-02) instead of the week prior; 0 of 32 records have more than one distinct valid_at, so 'adopted this cute pup from a shelter last week', 'started going to boot camps with my fam last month' are all misdated to conversation time. Every LoCoMo temporal question ('when did John graduate?') and value_as_of/fact_history time travel returns the wrong date by up to a month.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: speaker attribution on adverb/expletive-initial first-person sentences (facts keyed to 'Just'/'It's'/'Keep' instead of the speaker)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Just started doing aerial yoga, it's great.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Extracted as '[event] Just | started | Just started doing aerial yoga' — the subject is the adverb 'Just', not Maria; likewise 'Just got back from a family road trip yesterday' is keyed to 'Just', not John. Roughly 360 claims (~17%) sit under junk subjects (It's 109, It 80, Your 32, Keep 26, Let's 25, ...), so subject-keyed structured_recall for 'what workout class did Maria start?' misses the fact entirely even though a claim technically exists.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: photo-share attribution (image content credited to the session id, not the person who shared it)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: [shares a photo: a photo of a white board with a list of workouts on it]
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: In mem_437bef93 this John-shared photo is stored as 'c2_session_13 | shares a photo a | ...' — sharer identity is dropped (inconsistently: one photo in the same record is credited to Maria). LoCoMo adversarial/multimodal questions ask who shared which image; with the subject collapsed to a session id those are unanswerable from the claim layer, and the same photo caption ('a dog sitting in the grass with a leash') appears under both Max and Shadow records with no owner disambiguation.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g6-r0 in artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex: 32/32 records extracted (39-119 claims each, none zero), 2,165 claims, 215 non-inferred edges. Two visible extraction tiers: a small clean LLM tier (semantic events/intervals/quantities plus well-typed edges like 'donations funded fire truck') and a dominant verbatim per-sentence tier - 1,818/2,165 (84%) are 'state' claims that quote whole dialogue lines ('John: Hey Maria!', 'Keep it up!'). This guarantees text-match recall but floods typed retrieval with noise and mis-keyed subjects. Quantity coverage is nominally thin (3 claims) but the corpus only has ~4 numeric lines and most are covered via quantity/interval claims ('3 times a week', 'Max 10 years'); the one miss is the girl 'around 8' at the shelter (mem_c554a518). The four listed classes are the consequential gaps: no negative/retraction claims (invalid_at used 0 times), no relative-date resolution (0 records with >1 distinct valid_at), adverb-subject mis-attribution (~360 claims), and photo-sharer loss. Verdict 'patchy': volume is high and most facts exist somewhere in verbatim form, but whole classes needed for verification-grade recall (negation, event-time, entity keying) are systematically absent from the typed layer.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: meeting-time proposals and the final agreed plan (appointments)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Jolene: How about Friday at 5? I will need to sort out the books from this bookcase and I will be free. ... Deborah: Absolutely, let's do that! Can't wait for our coffee date next week.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: In mem_4c16157ef8574fe8 the coffee-date negotiation (Monday proposal -> 'How about Wednesday at 4?' -> Deborah retracts 'I already have plans' -> agreed 'Friday at 5') produced ZERO claims containing Wednesday/Friday/4/5 — only the vague 'Wanna meet up at that cafe next Monday?' sentence-claim survives. 'When did they agree to meet?' is a classic LoCoMo question; the store can only cite the superseded Monday proposal, so verify-or-abstain will either abstain or verify the WRONG (retracted) time. This is also a missed in-conversation supersession chain the bi-temporal machinery exists for.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: event locations attached to dated events
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: This pendant reminds me of my mother, she gave it to me in 2010  in Paris.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: mem_68b8c92b10474762: the structured claim captured the year (Jolene's mother | gave pendant to Jolene | 2010) and the edge 'received pendant from', but 'Paris' appears in zero claims and zero edges (the sentence-level shred also missed this line entirely). Where-questions about this event cannot be answered or cited; the double-space typo ('2010  in Paris') likely broke the extractor's span.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: relative-date resolution for dated events (event time vs session time)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Jolene: Two weeks ago I read "Avalanche" by Neal Stephenson in one sitting!
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: In mem_fb99286bfb3449c2, 'last week' (milestone), 'two weeks ago' (Avalanche), and 'last summer in Bogota' all have claims, but every claim's valid_at == the session timestamp 1675532880 (2023-02-04). Event time is never resolved, so value_as_of/fact_history time-travel and cross-session 'when did X happen' ordering collapse all pre-session events onto the conversation date; only a free-text reader can recover the offset.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: first-person preferences/habits filed under non-entity subjects (speaker attribution lost)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Jolene: Glimpsing that calms me. Taking beach walks relaxes me.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: mem_67872955013a49b4: 'Taking beach walks relaxes me' exists only as claim subject='Taking' (likewise 'Sooo excited!' -> subject='Sooo', 'You've got this!' -> subject='You've'). The fact is stored but unreachable via entity-keyed paths (preference_profile, structured_recall subject=Jolene), so 'What relaxes Jolene?' misses a preference that is verbatim in the store.

raw_extraction.notes: Store opened fine with DATA_DIR=artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data; counts match target exactly (30 records / 1787 claims / 204 edges). Sampled 5 records in depth — longest mem_fb99286bfb3449c2 (6385 chars, 130 claims, 18 edges), shortest mem_67872955013a49b4 (1573, 38, 0), mem_4c16157ef8574fe8 (2212, 43, 0), mem_68b8c92b10474762 (2028, 38, 6), zero-edge mem_0df5a0f51c17494b (2917, 55, 0) — plus a full-namespace sweep of all 30 records checking every sentence containing digits or negation phrases against claim text. Extraction style is exhaustive sentence-shredding (~1 claim per sentence, proof_atom = source sentence), so raw coverage is near-total: the sweep flagged only 5 uncovered sentences namespace-wide, and key entities on the longest record (Avalanche, Sapiens, Bogota, Anna, water purifier, amulet, pendant) all have both claims and typed edges. Typed extraction also includes interval (13) and quantity (7) claims (durations '3 years', age 'Max=8', wedding '1993'), and the 7 zero-edge records are not real gaps — their relationships (e.g. Jolene has_partner) were deduped into edges from other records. Claim-type mix: state 1474 / event 163 / preference 130 / interval 13 / quantity 7. So health = healthy, but the four listed classes are systematic: appointment times are the one outright hole (all three time proposals in the coffee-date negotiation extracted to nothing), event locations can silently drop ('Paris'), all claims inherit session valid_at (no relative-date grounding — every 'last week/last summer' event is bi-temporally keyed to the chat date), and gerund/interjection-initial first-person sentences get non-entity subjects, hiding preferences from entity-keyed recall.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Lost my job as a banker yesterday, so I'm gonna take a shot at starting my own business.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 of 1196 claims have valid_at different from the record's session timestamp -- 'yesterday', 'last Friday', 'a few years ago', 'last year', 'next month' (24+ occurrences across the 19 records) are never resolved to event time. Bi-temporal recall (value_as_of, fact_history) and any 'when did X happen' LoCoMo temporal question cannot be answered deterministically; every fact is dated to the conversation, not the event.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: my fav memory was when my team won first place at a regionals at age fifteen
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Exactly 1 of 1196 claims carries a unit (value='fifteen', unit='years old'); zero claims have a numeric value field. Counts, ordinals ('first place'), and durations ('a few days') exist only as prose. This is the known verify-or-abstain weak spot (measure_sum / numeric aggregation) -- with no typed numeric substrate, count questions fall back to NLI over sentences, which cannot catch arithmetic or scope errors.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: second-person / coreference attribution (facts about the listener)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: You never shy away from a challenge and always try something new.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: This is Jon describing Gina, but the claim subject is 'You'. 403/1196 claims (34%) have non-person subjects ('Your', "It's", 'Keep', 'BTW', 'Looks', 'Chase') because the extractor takes the sentence's first token as subject. Entity-keyed recall (preference_profile, fact_history, subject-filtered structured_recall for 'Gina') silently misses a third of the store; traits stated in second person are unfindable by name.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: We do all kinds of dances, from contemporary to hip-hop.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Lists are stored as one blob claim (here a single 'interval' claim), never split into per-item claims ('studio teaches contemporary', 'studio teaches hip-hop'). Enumeration questions cannot be answered via the deterministic claim path and force the expensive free-read path. Consistent with the itemized-list claim family only landing in the 2026-07-04 cost blitz -- this r13 store predates it.

raw_extraction.notes: Store at artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data, namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g8-r0: 19 records / 1196 claims / 99 edges confirmed. Sampled 8 records spanning longest (mem_c735, 4516 chars, 90 claims) to shortest (mem_6e07, 1525 chars, 40 claims); every record has 40-90 claims and every number/date-bearing sentence I scanned is present verbatim in some claim value -- so this is NOT dead extraction. Verdict is patchy because the misses are structural (whole classes), not spotty: (1) all 1196 claims share the record's session valid_at, (2) 1 unit-typed claim total, (3) 34% junk subjects from first-token-as-subject parsing, (4) no list decomposition. Claim-type mix is 89% 'state' near-verbatim sentence echoes including pure filler ('Gina: Wow!', 'Jon: Ha, ha!' as state claims), which inflates the 1196 count and pollutes retrieval ranking. The 99 edges are markedly cleaner than the claims (typed relations: lost job x7, owns x5, won first place x4, has profession x4) and DO capture the key biographical facts, but 6 are pruned and one pruned edge ('Marley flooring') has invalid_at==valid_at making it never-valid. Substantive biography (both job losses incl. Door Dash, banker, dance studio, online clothing store, regionals win) is recoverable via text-NLI, but temporal, numeric, listener-attributed, and enumerable facts have no deterministic/typed representation.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: 25 minutes on, then 5 minutes off for something fun.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only 6 quantity claims exist in 2266 (1 with a parseable numeric value); this one was stored as subj='c4_session_18' pred='25 minutes on then' val=<whole sentence> unit=''. Numeric aggregation (measure_sum) and verified counting cannot operate on sentence-echo values -- exactly the failure family behind the 2026-07-09 'verify-or-abstain leaks on numeric aggregation' headline.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: relative dated events resolved to absolute dates
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Last week I got this amazing deal with a renowned outdoor gear company.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 36 relative-date phrases (last week/last month/next month) appear in record text but only 6 claims carry a resolved ISO date. The endorsement-deal record (mem_38032b68bf0749d3) has the event claim with the raw sentence as value, no resolved date, and 0 edges -- 'when did John get the gear deal?' style temporal questions and as_of time-travel cannot be answered from typed claims.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Anthony and I went to this charity thing and ended up in this intense Harry Potter trivia contest.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Anthony appears in 0 claims (as subject or object) and 0 edges namespace-wide; the event claim is subj='John' pred='went' obj='charity thing'. LoCoMo multi-hop questions frequently pivot on secondary people ('who went with John?'), and entity-keyed retrieval/graph traversal has nothing to hit.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: negations and hypotheticals (extracted inverted as positive facts)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I haven't been to Edinburgh yet, but it definitely sounds like a place worth considering for our trip.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: This became [preference] subj='John' pred='like' obj='Edinburgh yet, ...' -- a negated visit stored as a positive preference; similarly the hypothetical 'It's like if I couldn't read due to an injury' became [event] Tim 'couldnt read due to an injury'. 37 negation markers exist in text; inverted claims are worse than missing ones because NLI verification can cite them to affirm the opposite of the source.

raw_extraction.notes: Store access: DATA_DIR=artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data, namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g9-r0 (29 records, 2266 claims, 231 edges confirmed). Sampled 8 records (shortest: mem_951b827c217b42bb, mem_e7e9d31945514933, mem_24c05ecab3a24245; mid: mem_38032b68bf0749d3, mem_9d2edf28e37b4392; longest: mem_ebebe88876af4b0e, mem_aa7c9978d67b402a, mem_8bee2da817d448a9). Coverage is broad, not dead: every record yields 41-132 claims and virtually every substantive sentence survives at least as a verbatim 'state' echo claim, so text-similarity recall is protected. But ~82% of claims (1858/2266 'state') are sentence echoes with junk subjects ('Keep', 'Take', \"It's\", 'Hope', 'Guess', 'c4_session_NN'), predicates that are just the first words of the sentence, and value=full sentence -- these add retrieval noise without typed structure. The well-typed minority is real (e.g. mem_ebebe88876af4b0e: favorite book/film-franchise preferences, knows-German/Spanish, Aragorn edges; 6 events did get ISO-resolved dates like Tim 'join fantasy literature forum' 2023-06-14), which is why I rate patchy rather than dead. Edges are uneven per record: 4 records have 0 edges (mem_38032b68bf0749d3 loses the outdoor-gear endorsement entirely from the graph; mem_9d2edf28e37b4392 loses the Edinburgh suggestion, Patrick Rothfuss recommendation, and The Alchemist), and several edges are duplicated verbatim (e.g. two identical 'Tim writes articles about fantasy novels' edges in mem_951b827c217b42bb) with odd self-superseding chains (Harry Potter vs Game of Thrones 'favors' edges superseding each other). Verification hazard worth flagging beyond the 4 classes: photo-caption lines are extracted as profession claims ([state] John has_profession 'baseball player' from '[shares a photo: a photo of a baseball player holding a bat next to a soda]'), i.e. image-alt text is treated as asserted fact.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: By the way, have you been to Italy? I had a blast there last month.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The claim exists ([state] John | visited | Italy | val='last month') but its valid_at is the SESSION date (2024-01-02), identical to record.valid_at — the event is not backdated to ~Dec 2023. Only 6 of ~36 relative-date mentions ('last week/month/summer/year') across the corpus got resolved ISO dates. This breaks the store's bi-temporal promise: as_of/value_as_of and any 'when did X happen' question answers the wrong era, and LoCoMo temporal-reasoning questions score against exactly these.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I got a no for a summer job I wanted which wasn't great but I'm staying positive.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Captured only as a verbatim sentence-shingle state claim, never as a typed negative assertion (Tim | NOT hired | summer job). The verify-or-abstain retraction path ('No — <premise>') keys on negation-typed claims; 'Did Tim get the summer job?' must instead survive NLI over a raw shingle, the exact failure mode already flagged in the 2026-07-09 headline (NLI misses scope/polarity). Same gap for 'Haven't been there yet' (John has never visited Universal Studios).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Planning a trip there next month. ... Nope, but it's my first time going. I'm super stoked for the Harry Potter stuff.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Tim's planned Universal Studios trip produced zero event claims and no resolved date ('next month' unanchored) — only shingle fallbacks stamped at session time. Past events with explicit weekday anchors DO get resolved (e.g. 'last Friday' → 2023-11-10 event claim), so the date resolver simply skips forward-looking expressions; any 'when is/was Tim's Universal Studios trip' recall has no typed event to cite.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Books, movies, and real-life experiences all fire up my creativity.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Stored only as one verbatim shingle ('books movies and real-life'); no per-item claims (Tim | inspiration source | books / movies / real-life experiences). The claim_list_enum / itemized-list P1b family from the cost blitz is entirely absent in this namespace (claim_type dist = state 1856 / preference 198 / event 178 / interval 22 / quantity 5, no list type). Multi-answer enumeration questions ('what inspires Tim's writing?') can't be answered by claim assembly and fall back to free-read. List splitting is inconsistent — 'family, exploring, hiking or playing board games' DID get split — so this is a coverage hole, not a missing capability.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g0-r0 (r14, LoCoMo dialog transcripts, Tim/John): 29 records all extracted, 45-136 claims each (~20 claims/KB), 2259 claims + 239 non-inferred edges. Two-layer extraction: (1) semantic typed claims — good coverage of preferences (198), quantities/durations (25/5-min pomodoro, 40 points, 4-yr teammates, 5-yr surfing all typed with units), person relationships (wife/dad/teammate/doctor edges), and 6 events with resolved ISO dates; (2) verbatim sentence-shingle fallback capturing nearly every sentence, so raw facts are almost never wholly absent — the misses above are structured/typed variants, which is what deterministic recall and verify-or-abstain need. Edge coverage is patchy: 5 of 29 records have 0 edges (mem_48811670, mem_e6686f9d, mem_db262782, mem_23b6090c, mem_3cb34b3d) and the named friend 'Anthony' exists only in claims, never as a graph edge. Separate PRECISION problem (not coverage): the fallback layer stores hypotheticals as facts ("It's like if I couldn't read due to an injury" became both an event claim 'Tim couldnt read due to an injury' and a preference), misparses ('gave a presentation' -> 'gift from Tim | presentation in class'), and junk subjects ("It's", "Hope", "Take", "c4_session_18") that pollute entity-keyed recall. Verdict healthy rather than patchy because most substantive facts have at least one claim and quantities/preferences/relationships are well typed; the single most consequential systematic gap is relative-date backdating (claim.valid_at == ingestion date for ~30/36 relative-date facts), which silently defeats the bi-temporal as_of machinery on this benchmark.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Deborah: Hey Jolene, sorry to tell you this but my dad passed away two days ago.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 of 1781 claims and 0 of 233 edges have valid_at different from the session timestamp -- the death edge is literally dst='two days ago' with valid_at=session time, and even 'This is my parents' wedding in 1993' gets valid_at=2023. The store's bi-temporal as_of/value_as_of machinery and every LOCOMO 'when did X happen' question answer with the wrong era; 'two weeks ago I read Avalanche', 'last summer in Bogota', 'bought a console on the 17th' are all frozen at ingest time.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: Scheduled plans / appointment times, including proposal-retraction-final chains
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Jolene: How about Friday at 5? I will need to sort out the books from this bookcase and I will be free. ... Deborah: Absolutely, let's do that!
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: In mem_aa9261fe35c04cac zero claims contain 'Wednesday', 'Friday', 'at 4', or 'at 5' -- the sentence splitter drops the 'How about DAY at TIME?' clauses entirely, keeping only the tails. The superseded Wednesday-at-4 proposal, its retraction ('Sorry, I remembered that I already have plans for this day' -- stored verbatim with unresolved 'this day'), and the agreed Friday-at-5 coffee date are unrecoverable, so update-tracking questions ('when are they meeting?') fail.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Jolene: We're not married yet but we've been together for three years.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The extractor lifts the duration (quantity: 'three years') but the 'not married' half exists only as a sentence-echo state claim with predicate 'jolene we re not' -- no typed negation (subject=Jolene, married=false). False-premise questions ('When did Jolene get married?') depend on a negation the retriever can match by entity+predicate; verbatim first-words predicates rank poorly, so verify-or-abstain will abstain (or worse, confabulate from 'been together for three years').

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: [shares a photo: a photo of a building with a curved roof on a hill]
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Photo-caption claims get subject = the session id (subj='c7_session_25', pred='shares a photo a') instead of the person who shared it; only a handful of edges ('Deborah --shares--> photo of a row of surfboards...') attribute sharer, and none in the sampled shortest record. LOCOMO's image-grounded and adversarial questions ('who shared the photo of X?') cannot be answered by claim lookup when the subject is a session token.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g1-r0 in artifacts/holdout_rotation_r14_codex: 30 records, 1781 claims, 233 edges confirmed. Extraction fires on every record (38-123 claims each, roughly proportional to length; sampled mem_b98b9a6372a24006 shortest 1573 chars/38 claims, mem_7494261bbf75429b longest 6385 chars/123 claims, plus mem_fa446928426040d7, mem_aa9261fe35c04cac, mem_e621998fca71413e, mem_f989695a085a4d8d, mem_4dc71ab71cd94446 probes). What WORKS: itemized lists are covered (Zelda BOTW / Animal Crossing / Overcooked 2 get both a combined 'recommends games' claim and per-item claims); pet ages get real quantity claims (Max=8/Luna=5, unit=years); durations get interval claims ('been together three years' val=3 unit=years); relationship edges are rich (owns, has mother, bonded with, married at, died, lost friend Karlie). What drags it to patchy: 1472/1781 (83%) of claims are near-verbatim sentence echoes with degenerate subjects ('Sooo', 'Take', 'It', \"That's\", 'Bye') and first-three-words predicates -- retrievable as text but useless for entity-predicate lookup; the 309 structured claims (165 event, 128 preference, 7 quantity, 9 interval) carry the real signal. The four missed classes above are systematic, not per-record: event-time resolution is absent namespace-wide (verified programmatically: every claim and edge valid_at == record session time within 60s), and the schedule-time clause drop was verified by full-claim dump of the scheduling record. Also noted but below the 4-class cap: quantities inside anecdotes ('I once won three large pizzas!') and Pomodoro numbers (25 min work / 5 min break) never become quantity claims (only 7 quantity claims in the whole namespace); a mis-attributed nickname claim (subj='Jolene' pred='nickname' obj='Deb' -- Deb is Deborah's nickname, used by Jolene) hints speaker-attribution noise in the structured extractor.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: dated events with resolved event time (relative-date anchoring)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I noticed they weren't acting normally, so I made an appointment with an animal behaviorist last Wed.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 of 2147 claims have valid_at different from the record timestamp -- the event claim stores value='last Wed' raw. The bitemporal index is flat, so as_of/when-questions (LoCoMo temporal reasoning) cannot resolve 'last Wed'/'yesterday'/'last weekend' to dates. One-off exception proves capability exists ([event] Andrew | start | val=2023-03-20 for the job) but even that claim's valid_at is record-time, so time-travel queries never see it.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: It's really inspiring how dedicated you are to making a difference, even though you can't volunteer at the shelter anymore.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Captured only as a sentence-shingle with subject 'It's' / predicate 'are to' (mem_6c6592a6e5df4738). No negation-typed claim, no claim carries invalid_at (0 namespace-wide), and no Audrey-volunteers edge was ever created or invalidated. Current-truth questions ('does Audrey volunteer?') can verify a stale positive or miss the retraction entirely -- exactly the verify-or-abstain leak class.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: speaker attribution on first-person / elliptical utterances
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Miss being outdoors. It's hard to find open spaces in the city. Used to hike a lot, but it's more challenging now with my work life balance.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: ~82% of claims are shingles whose subject is the sentence's first token: [state] Miss | miss being outdoors, [state] It's | it s hard to, [state] Can't | can t beat being. These are Andrew's facts but are not keyed to Andrew, so entity-keyed retrieval ('what does Andrew miss?', 'why did Andrew stop hiking?') misses them and the entity index is polluted with junk subjects (It's, Here's, Take, Talk).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Used to hike a lot, but it's more challenging now with my work life balance.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: No typed claim or edge distinguishes past-habit from current-habit (no ended interval, no supersession). Meanwhile other records assert Andrew hikes (edges 'hiked', 'hikes', 'enjoys hiking with dog'), so 'does Andrew still hike regularly?' resolves to an unqualified yes; the frequency-change fact exists only inside an unattributed shingle.

raw_extraction.notes: Extraction volume is high and text coverage dense: 28/28 records have claims (43-117 each), so text-match recall and NLI verification against claim values still function. The typed layer genuinely works for salient relational facts: itemized lists fully decomposed (all 4 dogs x breed via 'Pepper and Panda are Lab mixes, and Precious and Pixie are Chihuahua mixes' -> 4 is-edges; all 4 dogs x favorite_spot from one sentence), adoption events, job title with one resolved date, quantities (Audrey has 4 dogs, 3-year ownership, 2-hour hike), and edge-level supersession is live (39 superseding, 57 expired edges). Rated patchy rather than healthy because two whole classes are structurally absent (event-time anchoring: 0/2147 claims; claim-level invalidation/negation: 0/2147) and ~82% of claims are sentence-shingles with corrupted subjects that dilute the entity index. Rated patchy rather than dead because the typed 20% covers most benchmark-salient facts. Store access: DATA_DIR=artifacts/holdout_rotation_r14_codex/data, namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g2-r0; sampled records mem_b9e6ca0a56024ad6 (shortest, 1916 chars, 43 claims, 0 edges, zero typed claims -- pure shingle dropout), mem_04b65beab8b546b9 (longest, 5466 chars, 117 claims, 8 edges), plus targeted probes on mem_ba5bc36c7a86481f (job), mem_6c6592a6e5df4738 (volunteer retraction), mem_d202b2fcd48d4a61 (Pixie), mem_04b65beab8b546b9 (Saturday-hike/Sunday-picnic plans, captured with weekday-only values). Edge count observed live was 247 vs 249 in the target manifest (2 likely expired/pruned).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: At only $10 per class, it's very cheap! (mem_327f51d0)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only 9 quantity claims exist across 2229, and the canonical one dropped the value: '[quantity] cooking class | costs | class' — the $10 survives only inside a raw-text state splat ('james at only 10'). Numeric how-much/how-many questions have no deterministic typed path; this is exactly the numeric-aggregation class where verify-or-abstain already leaks (5/13 verified-WRONG headline).

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I never liked cooking, but I felt that I wanted to learn something new. (mem_327f51d0); I still haven't gotten a dog, but I still really want one. (mem_4bdb2555)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Zero negation-typed claims vs 42 negation-word occurrences in record texts, and polarity gets INVERTED: extraction produced '[preference] James | liked | cooking'. John's no-dog fact exists only as an untyped sentence splat. Recall can assert the opposite of the source, and the first-class 'No — <premise>' retraction promise has nothing to fire on.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I adopted a pup from a shelter in Stamford last week... I named it Ned. (mem_bd438570); three days ago I bought myself an adventure book (mem_d234ead5)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The Ned adoption edge/claim valid_at equals the session timestamp exactly (delta 0.0 days); event_time is None everywhere. 'last week'/'three days ago' offsets are never applied, so when-did-X and as_of time-travel answers are wrong by up to a week and cross-session temporal ordering is unreliable.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: extracted-then-expired facts (wrongful supersession deactivates unrelated edges)
