missed_fact_classes.fact_class: dated events with RESOLVED event-time (relative dates like 'last June' never converted to claim valid_at)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I was thinking of Costa Rica, my cousin got married there last June and the pictures looked amazing.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0/51 records have more than one distinct claim valid_at -- every claim inherits the session ingest timestamp (e.g. all 97+ claims of mem_51dae231 carry valid_at 1684914840.0). The wedding is stored only as a state slice; there is no event claim dated to June of the prior year. Bi-temporal recall (as_of, value_as_of, fact_history) and LongMemEval temporal-reasoning questions ('when did X happen', 'what happened first') operate on valid_at, so relative-dated events are unqueryable by time even though the text is present.

Worse, the sentence slices that do capture these facts get bogus subjects (the cousin-marriage claim has subject='Eco-Lodges'), so multi-hop 'whose/which relative' questions cannot be answered structurally and must fall back to full-text NLI over claim values.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: user-attributed preferences (preference claims exist but subject attribution is broken)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: user: I'd love to discuss potential applications in my project.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 380 preference claims exist, but sampled ones are keyed to wrong subjects: this user preference is stored with subject='Healthcare'; the user's peak-hours posting habit in mem_66d8b6e8 is stored under subject='IGTV'; list items from the ASSISTANT's recommendations are typed as preferences (subject='f75238a5_2', predicate='like'). Any preference_profile-style lookup keyed on the user returns noise or misses, and assistant-content pollution inflates the preference class with facts the user never expressed.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: 1. **The AI Alignment Podcast**: A fantastic podcast that delves into the latest AI research, applications, and implications.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: List items are each captured as separate state/preference claims, but the leading ordinal is stripped from the claim value (e.g. value begins '**AI conferences and meetups**...') and no list-membership/position structure is stored. Questions like 'what was the third recommendation' or 'how many resources were suggested' cannot be answered from claims without re-reading the raw record; this matches the known ordinal-list backlog item from the wave-G audit.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g12-r0 (DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data): 51 records, 5245 claims, 10 edges. Extraction is dense and near-exhaustive: 51/51 records have claims (min 2, median 97, max 208 per record, scaling with text length). Sentence-level substring audit on 6 sampled records (398 to 21,272 chars) shows the only systematically skipped sentences are interrogatives (questions), and facts embedded in questions (e.g. 'peak hours 12pm-3pm') also appear in captured declarative sentences -- so raw fact coverage is high. Typed extraction works where triggered: quantity claims carry units (subj='AI-powered systems', pred='accuracy rate in detecting breast cancer', unit='percent', val='97'), durations get interval claims (subj='conversation', pred='lasted', unit='minutes', val='10'), markdown tables get table claims preserving cell values ('| Month | NYC to LAX (Trip 1) | $500 | ...'). Negations are present verbatim in state slices; no genuine user retractions exist in this window (all negation hits are assistant boilerplate 'I don't have personal experiences'), so no retraction-class gap is demonstrable here. The four listed misses are structural rather than raw-coverage gaps: the dominant claim style is verbatim sentence slicing (4491/5245 are 'state' claims) with frequently wrong subject/predicate headers, uniform valid_at = session ingest time (0/51 records show any event-time resolution), and no entity-relationship edges. Net: text recall via NLI over claim values should work well; anything relying on claim structure -- temporal as_of queries, subject-keyed lookup, relationship hops, ordinals -- silently degrades to full-text fallback.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: By the way, I recently got invited to a writing group meetup by a friend, Rachel, which I'm really looking forward to.

The Rachel fact exists only as a heuristic claim keyed to subject 'Prompts' (a section header), so any entity-keyed or multi-session join query (7 multi-session questions in this window) cannot reach it through the graph.

Relative anchors like 'next week' / 'recently' never got normalized, so EVENT_CHAIN_CONTEXT, temporal rerank and the 8 temporal-reasoning questions in this window have no calendar substrate to reason over.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: the median annual wage for civil engineers in May 2020 was $87,060 for those with a Bachelor's degree, while those with a Master's degree earned a higher median annual wage of $113,300.

missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The fact is captured but keyed subject='Tools', predicate='user i m a' (heuristic picked a nearby section header). Top claim subjects are '76385c25', 'sharegpt_zhBMAT7_21', 'Prompts' — so 'who/what is the user' queries (4 single-session-user questions here, plus employer NovaTech) cannot be answered through the typed-claim index; only raw-text retrieval saves them.

raw_extraction.zero_extraction_cause: Edges (and events/entities) are zero because the sleep-time LLM extraction budget collapsed to raw-only for this whole batch — not a crash and not extraction never running. The holdout profile (eidetic/config.py:63-64) sets CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_CALL_BUDGET=90 and CONSOLIDATION_LONG_HAYSTACK_RAW_ONLY=1; in Engine.consolidate_pending (eidetic/engine.py:1436-1465) g13's 44 mostly >6k-char records planned more chunk windows than the 0.8*90 trigger, so raw_only_long_haystack=True set every record's window allowance to 0, skipping ALL extract_edges/extract_claims LLM calls -> zero triples -> zero graph.add_fact edges, zero EventRecords, entities=[] on all 44 records. Proof: 43/44 records carry metadata consolidation_raw_only='batch_window_budget' (the 44th, mem_881e39df4dc14768, drained in an earlier/smaller batch but still yielded no triples). Sibling namespaces in the SAME sqlite got edges (g1:103, g14:152, g10:88), so the pipeline works when the batch fits the budget. The 4,970 claims survive because claims_for_record (eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py:1257) falls back to deterministic heuristic_claims_from_text, which needs no LLM. Fix path: re-run sleep for g13 under BENCH_FULL_CONSOLIDATION_PROFILE (config.py:87-98, budget=0/raw_only=0) or raise the call budget.
raw_extraction.notes: Target metadata was partially wrong: the namespace has 4,970 claims (all 44 records covered, min/med/max 13/109/213 per record), not zero — only edges/events/entities are zero. Sampled 6 records (871, 2019, 10742, 17593, 19442, 21508 chars). Claim-type distribution: state 4263, preference 319, quantity 144, interval 116, event 97, table 31. Claims are verbatim-sentence proof atoms from the deterministic heuristic extractor, so NLI verify-or-abstain over proof atoms still works, but the STRUCTURED channel is hollow: subjects are section headers/session ids ('76385c25' 149x, 'sharegpt_zhBMAT7_21' 99x, 'Prompts', 'Tools'), 0/144 quantity claims have parsed numeric value or unit, only 18/97 event claims carry event_date, 0 negation-flagged claims, 0 edges/events in sqlite for this namespace (verified directly). Health is 'patchy' rather than 'dead' because sentence-level claim coverage is 100% of records and quotes are recallable lexically; it is 'dead' if judged on the graph/typed channel alone. Key files: eidetic/engine.py:1436-1533 (budget gating + _extract), eidetic/config.py:62-98 (holdout defaults vs BENCH_FULL_CONSOLIDATION_PROFILE), eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py:1257 (claims_for_record heuristic fallback). Store: artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data/eidetic.sqlite.

The bi-temporal machinery (as_of recall, value_as_of, fact_history, temporal-ordering questions in LongMemEval) gets no event-time signal at all -- 'when did X happen' and era-scoped queries can only ever resolve to session time.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: user: I'm actually considering a gas grill, and my budget is around $500-$700 --> extracted quantity claim: subject='Ingredients', predicate='is around', value=<the whole sentence>, unit=''; similarly study-hours claim got subject='Creams'

This is exactly the substrate the measure_sum/deterministic-aggregation path sums over -- it directly feeds the known live failure (5/13 verified-WRONG, 1226.3-vs-70 over-sum) because unrelated garbage-subject quantities are indistinguishable from real ones.

raw_extraction.notes: Verbatim text-level coverage is dense and healthy: all 46 records have claims (4-244 each, median ~120; roughly 1 claim per 90 chars), and a sentence-scan across 8 sampled records (shortest 140B to longest 19.8KB) found essentially zero number- or negation-bearing sentences without a covering claim; kin mentions covered 6/6 records. The 'patchy' verdict is about the TYPED layer, not raw capture: claim_type mix is state 4585 / preference 392 / interval 161 / event 135 / quantity 131, and the four classes above are structurally absent or degraded even though their surface text was captured as state claims. Practical read: free-read/NLI recall over this namespace should work; the deterministic paths (value_as_of, structured_recall aggregation, as_of time travel, bridge-join) are starved. Repro: DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data, Engine().store on namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g14-r0; key measurements: zero claims with |valid_at - record.valid_at| > 1h; claims_in_scope shows 0 invalid_at; quantity claims 102/131 unitless, 30/131 clean-numeric; edges concentrated in 7 records (mem_450fad8e alone has 50 of 139). Note: all_edges(include_inferred=False) returns 139, not the 152 in the target line -- the extra 13 are presumably inferred edges.

LongMemEval temporal questions ('when did X happen', 'how old was the user on date D') and value_as_of/fact_history time-travel cannot resolve — the only date the user ever stated explicitly is unrecoverable from structured fields.

This is the exact substrate gap behind the 2026-07-09 headline (measure_sum over-count, 1226.3-vs-70 shipping verified=True): deterministic numeric aggregation has no normalized numbers to sum or verify against, so arithmetic/scope errors pass NLI.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g15-r0 (DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data): 51 records, 5116 claims (all with source_memory_id; 51 distinct sources; min 1 / median 104 / max 206 claims per record; 0 records with zero claims), 15 edges. Claim-type mix: state 4428, preference 340, interval 177, event 102, quantity 47, table 22. Extraction volume is healthy — the failure mode is structure, not coverage: (a) valid_at is uniformly the session timestamp, never the in-text event date; (b) ~50% junk subjects break entity keying; (c) quantity normalization is effectively absent (1/5116 units); (d) edge extraction ran on exactly one record. Secondary observation, not in top-4: retractions are unmodeled — 0 claims have invalid_at set anywhere in the namespace, though the 4 'no longer' record hits here are mostly generic assistant prose rather than user retractions, so low consequence for this window. Sampled records: mem_fc73055c198d44fc (116 ch, 1 claim), mem_c313831e9336482c, mem_bd2c71d87a9c4b18, mem_1e8902b0baf64e54 (median), mem_49db76024ae249b2 (21479 ch, 185 claims), mem_7a8889517bb34bc8, mem_7fbfc95d9df44d26. Note claims expose `predicate` (populated, noisy: 'like' 189, 'are some' 77...) not `relation`; the `value` field is overloaded with the raw source sentence for non-quantity claims.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: event-time normalization (dated events keep session time, not stated date)

Deterministic temporal paths (value_as_of, fact_history, temporal-reasoning ordering) will place the event at session time; only the NLI/reader path can recover the real date.

Deterministic numeric aggregation (measure_sum) over these must skip or mis-parse them -- directly feeds the open #41/#42 verified-wrong numeric-aggregation failure class.

raw_extraction.notes: Verified store counts match target: 55 records, 5297 claims, 116 edges (manifest said 118). No dead records: every record has >=2 claims (median ~85, max 225); the low-claim records (2-8 claims) are ShareGPT filler with little persona content anyway. Text-level capture is near-total: a systematic diff of all 63 user-turn numeric/date facts against claim text INCLUDING the value field found 0 misses (an earlier s/p/o-only scan showed 2 apparent misses -- '20 likes' and 'every other week since January' -- both actually present in value/claims), and both user relationship mentions are covered. Claim-type mix: state 4409, preference 362, interval 194, quantity 163, event 97, table 72. So extraction is HEALTHY on capture but weak on NORMALIZATION: (1) anchors empty on 100% of claims and event valid_at inherits session time, so in-text dates are text-only; (2) 70% of quantity claims carry sentences not numbers in value (unit set on only 49); (3) 51% junk/hash subjects break entity-keyed access; (4) edge graph is encyclopedic-skewed with near-zero persona coverage. invalid_at=0 across all claims (no supersession events observed; no clear user retractions found in record text, so not counted as a miss). Net effect: free-read/NLI recall should work well on this namespace; deterministic paths (structured_recall, value_as_of, measure sums, graph joins) are the ones exposed to the four classes above. Reproduce with: DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data, namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g16-r0; key exemplar memory_ids: mem_ebd28eb1b84e4b31 (Maya/Feb 22), mem_916125cc981f48a5 (plane every other week), mem_fcbc4883899e4530 (20 likes), mem_b687d4cb44ed462a (sister).

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: entity-relation edges (relationships/possession) — graph channel entirely dead

missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 edges and entities=[] on all 44 records means multi-hop/bridge-join traversal, PPR retrieval features, value_as_of/fact_history time travel, and graph.add_fact supersession (knowledge-update closing) are all inert for this namespace — any LongMemEval multi-session or knowledge-update question that needs entity linkage falls back to raw text search.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: dated events — temporal event index empty, event claims mostly undated with junk subjects

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: quantities/prices without parsed numeric value or unit
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: By the way, I booked a round-trip economy ticket from JFK to London on British Airways for $450, which I thought was a great deal. (mem_93ac1dd664)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The quantity claim for this fact has subject='Egypt' (wrong entity), value=<the whole raw sentence string>, unit='', filters.untyped=1 — deterministic SMQE numeric aggregation (sum/count) cannot operate on sentence-string values, which feeds directly into the known #41/#42 measure_sum over-count/wrong-sum failure mode.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: speaker attribution — assistant turns extracted as user preferences/facts
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: You're a detail-oriented owner, I like that. (assistant turn, stored as a preference profile line; also 'As an AI language model, I don't have personal preferences...')
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 424 preference claims and 47 profile lines include assistant-authored boilerplate attributed to the user; preference questions can be answered—and NLI-verified—against assistant text, producing confidently-cited wrong answers about what the USER prefers.

raw_extraction.notes: Premise correction: this namespace does NOT have zero claims — it has 4908 active claims, all mapped 1:1 to the 44 records (31–197 claims/record, roughly per-sentence). Only the EDGE/event/entity channel is zero. Root cause (zero_extraction_cause): all 44 records were consolidated with metadata.consolidation_raw_only='batch_window_budget' — the sleep-time LLM extraction call budget (consolidation_extract_call_budget, engine.py ~1436–1523) was exhausted for the batch, so window_allowance=0 for every record, _extract returned no triples, and graph.add_fact/store.add_event never ran (0 edges, 0 EventRecords, entities=[] everywhere). The 4908 claims all come from the deterministic heuristic_claims_from_text fallback in eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py:1257, which runs regardless of budget. Consequence profile: recall that can be answered by sentence-level text match is well covered (spot-checked 25 salient user facts: 24/25 had a covering claim), but every TYPED channel is degraded — claim subjects are junk ('Step','Way','If','Egypt' for a $450 flight), quantity values are raw sentence strings (unit=''), event claims mostly lack event_date, claim_type distribution is 4047 state / 424 preference / 141 interval / 123 event / 120 quantity / 53 table with filters.untyped=1 common. Fix path: re-run consolidation for this namespace with a raised consolidation_extract_call_budget or consolidation_raw_only_window_threshold (or EXTRACT_COMBINED to halve calls) — records are marked pending_consolidation=False so a re-sweep needs to re-flag or use repair.
raw_extraction.zero_extraction_cause: Edges (not claims) are zero: batch LLM extraction was budget-starved — all 44 records consolidated raw-only (metadata.consolidation_raw_only='batch_window_budget'; planned windows exceeded consolidation_extract_call_budget in engine.py consolidate_pending), so window_allowance=0, extract_edges never ran, graph.add_fact/add_event never fired. The 4908 claims are entirely from the deterministic LLM-free heuristic_claims_from_text fallback, which is budget-independent.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: I'm planning a trip to Japan in March for the Cherry Blossom season and I'm trying to decide on accommodations.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 of 5,154 claims have anchors; all 43 records' claims inherit the session valid_at verbatim (0 claims shifted >1 day, 0 records with more than one valid_at) even though 9 records contain relative-date phrases (yesterday/last week/next month). 'In March' survives only as a raw sentence chunk under subject='Harajuku'. LongMemEval temporal-reasoning questions ('when did the user...') and as_of time-travel cannot be answered from claims, and verification against event time is impossible.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: - I can spend up to $35,000 on this purchase
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: This budget claim (mem_89b892753fdc4374) got subject='Outback'; 208 claims namespace-wide have first-person proof atoms but subject != 'user' (e.g. subject='Beatles' for "I think I'll start with the 1960s", subject='Harajuku' for the user's trip plans). Any recall keyed on subject='user' ('what is the user's budget?') misses the user's own stated facts, and verification attributes them to the wrong entity.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: As context, I often drive with my family around New York City, and also drive 200 miles weekly through upstate New York roads.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: All 16 edges in the namespace come from a single record (mem_05baf0c92f1240b4, music genres), and 10 of the 16 are pruned=True. The other 42 records — including 3 that mention family relations — produced zero edges, so graph traversal / multi-hop joins across sessions have nothing to walk.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: like my new reusable beeswax wraps that I've been using instead of plastic wrap
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: 0 of 5,154 claims have invalid_at set and none supersede another, despite 6 records containing 'instead of / no longer / switched' knowledge updates. Worse, negated or hedged text gets inverted into false 'preference' claims via the token 'like': subject='user' predicate='like' object="there's always a new conflict or crisis happening in the Middle East" (from 'It seems like...'). Knowledge-update questions will answer with stale facts because latest-assertion-wins never triggers, and verify-or-abstain has no superseded marker to check.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g18-r0 (43 records / 5,154 claims / 16 edges) shows DENSE but DEGENERATE extraction: ~120 claims per record, 4,371/5,154 typed 'state', and 4,076 filters tagged 'untyped'. The extractor is behaving as a syntactic sentence-chunker: subjects are sentence-initial tokens ('By', 'For', 'Here', 'You', 'The', \"It's\", 'May', 'If' are among the top subjects) and predicates are the first few lowercased words of the sentence ('user please recommend a', 'assistant i cannot make'). Substring coverage is therefore high — $30, $50, ¥1,070, 'March', 'Yoyogi' from the Japan-trip record all appear inside some claim's proof_atom/object — so nothing is zero-extracted, ruling out 'dead'. But as TYPED, verifiable facts, four whole classes are missing (temporal anchors, correct speaker attribution, relationship edges, retraction/supersession), which is the definition of 'patchy'. Quantity claims (114) carry raw sentence text in value/unit fields (e.g. value='- I ca...') rather than parsed numbers — directly feeding the known measure_sum over-counting failure (5/13 verified-WRONG on live LME-S). Sampled records: mem_4f4c2ef5beee448a, mem_89b892753fdc4374, mem_963c18518a98459b, mem_4e0e211571b74ec6, mem_445fcfa96db04783, mem_0e4620bc9d0d4bd3, mem_c0cf9a8bb0504b72, mem_87bb5ded61d14cab; full dump at /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-raunakgengiti-Eidetic-Plus/eb09f70d-9fc9-40be-884b-dc6507a26ca8/scratchpad/sample.txt. Record text lives in MemoryRecord.text (not .content).
raw_extraction.zero_extraction_cause: n/a — extraction ran on all 43 records; the near-zero component is EDGE extraction (16 edges, all from 1 record, 10/16 pruned)

raw_extraction.description: Extraction coverage audit for namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g19-r0

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: The global market for sustainable fashion is expected to reach $9.81 billion by 2025 ... the market for banana fiber textiles expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.7% from 2020 to 2025 ... the market expected to reach $7.3 billion by 2027 (mem_82095993)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Sentence-splitting breaks on the decimal point, so the stored quantity claim is literally 'expected to reach $9' (also '$5', '$7', 'CAGR of 6') -- the magnitude, unit and year are severed. The truncated figure still NLI-entails, so verify-or-abstain cannot flag it. This is the exact numeric-aggregation leak the project already flagged as its headline, reproduced at the extraction layer: any count/sum/comparison recall reads a corrupted number.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: it is generally agreed that she married John Rolfe, an English colonist, in 1614 ... Thomas Rolfe, who was born in 1615 (mem_6bb7ec16)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The event claim is '[event] Pocahontas | married | John Rolfe' -- the year 1614 is dropped; the son's birth is misclassified as a quantity 'born | Thomas Rolfe' with no 1615. A bi-temporal store's whole selling point is valid_at / as_of time-travel, but event claims carry no date, so 'when did X happen' / as-of-year queries cannot resolve from the typed claims.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Dr Minkoff is a medical doctor who runs a large natural medicine clinic in Florida ... He also owns a nutrition company called Body Health ... He is an Ironman athlete and has done 43 of them (mem_d65e2796)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only 46 edges exist and they are concentrated in 6 of 50 records (all encyclopedic: Pocahontas, Obama, creatine); the other 44 conversational records produced ZERO edges. Person-org and person-attribute relationships survive only as garbled 'He | is an | an Ironman...' state fragments, so value_as_of / fact_history entity-relation traversal is dead for 88% of the corpus.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: Itemized / enumerated list members (survey stats, spec rows)
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: According to a survey, 75% of millennials are willing to pay more for sustainable clothing ... The handloom industry in India employs over 43 lakh weavers and artisans (mem_82095993)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: List items collapse into one malformed triple whose SUBJECT is the raw memory id ('sharegpt_WXCvFir_11 | are willing | a survey, 75% of millennials...'), with the count buried inside a truncated object. Each enumerated fact is not individually addressable, so counting/'how many'/list-recall queries cannot enumerate or verify individual members.

raw_extraction.notes:
Store loaded OK: 50 records, 5087 active claims, 46 edges (matches target). Claim-type mix: state 4312, preference 342, interval 143, table 105, event 102, quantity 83. Sampled records (shortest->longest): mem_733262d8 (230B, 2 claims), mem_6bb7ec16 (1916B, 20), mem_82095993 (11598B, 73), mem_d65e2796 (12126B, 35), plus type/edge distributions store-wide.

Verdict = patchy, not dead: extraction FIRES heavily (avg ~102 claims/record) and some facts are clean ('Barack Obama | born | August 4, 1961', 'Audio-Technica AT-LP60BK | costs | $99'). But two systemic defects gut usability: (1) pervasive triple malformation -- subjects are sentence-fragment tokens ('Versatility','However','There','As','By') or the raw source id ('sharegpt_ki9IVDq_6','a2053427_1'), predicates are raw text spans ('assistant certainly','is being','are you'), objects truncated mid-sentence at ~70-120 chars; (2) whole high-value classes lost -- numeric magnitudes severed at the decimal, event dates dropped, and relationship edges exist for only 6/50 records. Near-dead records also present: mem_733262d8 (a real user question -> 2 junk state claims) and the 12KB conversational mem_d65e2796 (35 claims, almost all garbled 'preference' fragments with no queryable subject). Negation handling is literal-text only (55 'not'-predicate claims, mostly boilerplate 'assistant i do not have personal beliefs'), no polarity structure.

Repro harness written: /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-raunakgengiti-Eidetic-Plus/eb09f70d-9fc9-40be-884b-dc6507a26ca8/scratchpad/audit.py (takes memory_id prefixes, dumps raw bytes via eng.get_raw(content_hash) + its claims). get_raw takes content_hash, not memory_id/scope.

Highest-leverage fix targets, in order: numeric truncation (sentence splitter breaks '$9.81' at the '.'), then subject/predicate resolution (stop emitting memory-ids and stopword tokens as subjects), then edge extraction for conversational records.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: "Photography Masterclass: Your Complete Guide to Photography" - 92% off, now $10.99 (originally $129)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Stored quantity claim object in mem_4edce3fc4e9440fc is literally 'Photography"** - 92% off, now $10' -- the value is clipped from $10.99 to $10 and '(originally $129)' is gone. A typed quantity claim with a wrong number is worse than no claim: the deterministic structured_recall/measure-sum path will return $10 with verified=True, exactly the #41/#42 numeric-aggregation leak class.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: dated events not typed or date-anchored
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Ajax has won the Eredivisie title in the past four seasons (2018-19, 2019-20, 2020-21, and 2021-22)
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: In mem_1959eddfa3ed4bde the phrase 'Eredivisie title' appears in NO claim (fact split across predicate 'ajax has won the' and object 'the past four seasons (...)', the title dropped). Only 101/5153 claims are 'event'-typed and those are mis-typed (funeral = 'interval'). Temporal tools (value_as_of, fact_history) cannot index what-happened-when if dates stay buried in free-text 'state' claims, and here the event's core noun phrase is lost outright.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: **Gradual integration**: After 2-3 days, start allowing the new birds to enter the main enclosure under close supervision
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: No claim from mem_f81b22c4dffd43f8 contains '2-3 days' -- this numbered step vanished while adjacent steps (2-3 weeks quarantine, 6-8 week weaners) were extracted; same pattern in mem_4edce3fc4e9440fc where price-list item 2 ('85% off, now $14.99') is absent while items 1/3/4/5 exist. Partial list extraction makes list-enumeration answers (the claim_list_enum carve-out) confidently incomplete: the reader sees N-1 items with citations and no signal one is missing.

missed_fact_classes.fact_class: person-relationship facts: no semantic edges, entity subjects garbled
missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: How my dad had just died from a heart-attack, and I would have to attend his funeral tomorrow and deliver a eulogy.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: mem_529c16d4c14b499c's death fact is stored as claim_type='interval' with subject='How'; all 10 edges in the namespace are 'co_activated' record-cooccurrence, zero person-person relation edges (dad/mom/brother relations never graphed). Verdict is patchy, not dead, because the STRUCTURED layer is unreliable: claim_type distribution is 86% 'state' (4438), preference 302, interval 160, quantity 149, event 101, table 3; typed claims are both sparse and error-prone (mis-typed events as intervals, quantity values truncated mid-number). Subject extraction is noisy: top subjects include 'The' (104), 'Here' (87), 'Recommendations' (83), 'Availability', 'However' -- 875/5153 claims keyed on pronouns/stopwords, breaking entity-indexed retrieval. Edges are effectively unextracted: 10 total, all relation='co_activated' with empty source_memory_id; no semantic relations at all. Retraction/negation language appears in 21/53 records but only as untyped state/preference claims (mostly generic prose here, so lower priority than the four classes reported). Note the get_raw API quirk: eng.get_raw(record.content_hash), not memory_id. Access script used: Engine + Scope exactly as given in the task.

raw_extraction.zero_extraction_cause: PREMISE CORRECTION: claims are NOT zero — 5148 claims exist and all 47 records have 15–234 claims each (min 15, max 234, zero uncovered records). Only the GRAPH channel is dead: 0 edges, 0 events, 0 entities. Root cause: batch window budget starvation. The holdout profile sets CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_CALL_BUDGET=90 (eidetic/config.py:63; confirmed '90' in artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/run_manifest.json). In the consolidation sweep (eidetic/engine.py ~1470-1530), 46/47 records carry metadata consolidation_raw_only='batch_window_budget' — their window_allowance was 0, so client.extract_edges was never called → 0 triples → graph.add_fact never ran → 0 edges, 0 EventRecords, 0 rec.entities. The single record that got allowance (mem_98bf6c9afd254ad4, 234 claims incl. LLM-validated interval/quantity) also yielded 0 triples (entities empty — its edge call either returned nothing or degraded silently via _degraded('extract-edges')). The 5148 claims survived because heuristic_claims_from_text in eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py (claims_for_record, line 1257) is deterministic and runs per-record regardless of the LLM window budget — hence sentence-granularity 'state' claims (4401 of 5148) with junk subjects but intact proof atoms. Also note: all 47 records still have consolidated=False and pending_consolidation=True, so a re-sleep with a higher/zero budget would retry LLM extraction.

raw_extraction.zero_extraction_cause: n/a — no zero extraction; all 48 records have claims (12–201 each, scaling with record length)
raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g26-r0 (artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex): 48 records / 5,222 claims / 10 edges. Extraction is a dense sentence-shredder: essentially every sentence becomes a claim with the full sentence preserved in proof_atom+value, so a namespace-wide regex probe found ALL user-turn mentions of dates (8), relationships (7), quantities (8), and negations (1) present verbatim in some claim atom of the same record — free-read/NLI paths can locate the text, hence not 'dead'. Rated patchy because whole STRUCTURED classes are absent or corrupted despite textual coverage: (1) claim_type mix is 4,485 state / 384 preference / 132 interval / 125 event / 95 quantity / 1 table — itemized lists shredded into per-item states, only 1 table claim; (2) ~26% of claims have junk subjects — top subjects include 'assistant', 'Here', 'The', \"It's\" and session hashes (8f3831ac, 726e9a1f_3, bb236214), making entity-keyed recall (value_as_of/fact_history) miss; (3) temporal anchoring dead: 0/48 records have more than one distinct valid_at (all claims inherit session date, e.g. 1684572660 = 2023-05-25); (4) 0 claims with invalid_at (no retraction/supersession machinery engaged); (5) edges are 10 co_activated pairs only, no semantic graph. Net effect: verification-by-quote works, but structured/deterministic recall paths (structured_recall, temporal as_of, preference_profile, aggregation) are answering from a noisy, session-time-flattened, assistant-contaminated claim layer. Sample dumps: /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-raunakgengiti-Eidetic-Plus/eb09f70d-9fc9-40be-884b-dc6507a26ca8/scratchpad/audit/

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: user: In addition to yoga, I've also been taking Pilates classes on Wednesday mornings to focus on core strengthening exercises... -> extracted as [state] Parking | in addition to yoga | ...
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only 129/5295 claims have subject=='user'; top subjects are chunk-ids ('1db471f5_1', 'sharegpt_fB2ri01_0'), stopword fragments ('The', "It's", 'In', 'With'), and wrong nouns ('Parking', 'Warm-up', 'Nordstrom', 'Festivals'). LongMemEval questions are all about the USER; any subject-keyed path (structured_recall, value_as_of, claim filtering by entity) cannot find these facts, and verification anchors to the wrong entity. Facts survive only as verbatim strings inside claim objects.

raw_extraction.notes: Verified counts: 50 records, 5295 claims, 161 non-inferred edges (manifest said 173; delta likely includes inferred). Extraction fires on every record (~1 claim/90 chars, min 4 claims on a 321-char record), and spot-probes for most benchmark-critical strings DO land somewhere in claim object text: 'November 12th', '27 minutes and 12 seconds', 'September 15th to 18th', '$15 H&M sundress' (clean: subject='yellow sundress', cost=$15), 'asylum... 18 months' (clean, subject='user'). So free-text/NLI recall over claim objects has raw material — not dead. But the structured layer is patchy: 84% of claims are type 'state' and a large share are verbatim sentence copies with junk subjects; claim_type distribution = state 4470, preference 329, quantity 185, interval 181, event 129, table 1. Low-density outliers (mem_df70b13122a24bf1, 52 claims/9125 chars) are benign repeated-story distractor sessions. Best-extracted record: mem_2aaa5eacca9e4bdd (sundress facts fully typed). Worst systemic failure: whatever picks claim subjects latches onto section headers / random capitalized nouns from assistant replies ('Parking', 'Warm-up' are literally headings in the assistant text) instead of the speaking user. No true negation/retraction turns found in this window (correction-scan hits were 'actually' as intensifier), so that class untested here. Repro: DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data, namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g27-r0; record text via eng.substrate.get(record.raw_uri.split('://')[1]).

raw_extraction.notes: Extraction is dense but shallow: 46/46 records have claims (~1 claim per 90 chars; 5 to 229 per record), and a sentence-vs-claim diff on the four lowest-density records (mem_65f13a36, mem_9d117997, mem_61df5bf2, mem_5012ae00) found zero substantive sentences without a matching shingle — so raw coverage for NLI verification is fine. The gaps are whole STRUCTURED classes: (1) 0/107 event claims re-anchored off session time, (2) 0 claims with invalid_at and no negation type, (3) edges from only 5/46 records with zero person relations, (4) 80/5454 claims subject='user'. Typed inventory: 4731 state / 328 preference / 146 quantity (only 31 with units) / 134 interval / 107 event / 8 table. Sampled records: mem_d8c6c58c (171ch, arithmetic correction), mem_8afcde7d (1.3k, askFundu), mem_4f9151cb (Melody Maker), mem_e9eb2ff0 (art museum), mem_61e94b84 (mom/dinner), mem_fdc0e67d (brother Charlie), mem_0bbc2b74 (safari lodges, edge-rich), mem_65f13a36/mem_9d117997/mem_61df5bf2 (low-density long). Verdict 'patchy' not 'healthy' because the deterministic/typed recall surfaces (time travel, retraction, graph hops, subject lookup) have near-zero substrate despite complete text capture; this is consistent with the known live headline that only the free-read path scores well.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g29-r0 (DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data): 45 records, 5303 active claims, 94 stored non-inferred edges (manifest says 111; 10 edges carry source_memory_ids not matching any record in the namespace). Extraction is ALIVE on every record — claims scale with length (349 chars→4 claims up to 20,491 chars→200 claims, roughly 10 claims/KB, no dead records), and every sampled LongMemEval evidence sentence (spending list $70/$120/$80+$20/$220/$50/$15, Instagram 127 likes/23 comments/25 followers, 1985 Hitchhiker's Guide $5, semester finished mid-June, Cyberpunk pre-order, Snaggletooth figure) is present verbatim inside some claim's value string, so NLI-verified free-read recall can still find them. Claim-type mix: state 4492, preference 296, quantity 214, interval 152, event 120, table 29. The patchiness is structural, not coverage: (1) uniform session-timestamp valid_at on 100% of claims (temporal normalization dead), (2) 188/214 quantity claims unparsed (feeds open #41/#42 over-sum bug), (3) sporadic list-item loss, (4) systematic subject misattribution (one dominant token or a claim-hash like 'ea8cc87b' as subject for most of a record) which nullifies entity-keyed/deterministic paths and edge building. Net: verbatim-retrieval paths are healthy; structured/deterministic paths (structured_recall, value_as_of, measure sums, graph edges) are the weak side for this namespace.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g3-r0 (window artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex): 46 records, 5,155 claims, 20 edges confirmed. Every record has claims (min 16, max 221), so extraction is not dead -- but it is a sentence-shingler, not a fact extractor: claim_type distribution is state 4485 / preference 315 / interval 146 / event 102 / quantity 80 / table 27, and most claims are verbatim sentence copies of BOTH user and assistant turns, including assistant boilerplate ('I hope you find them useful!'), inflating volume without fact value. The preference class is lexically triggered noise: 'Teach Like a Champion 2.0' -> preference 'like'; assistant's 'I'm happy to help' -> preference. Quantities/durations DO get extracted well when explicit (teacher counts, '$3,500', '11 years' with value/unit fields), and itemized lists are covered line-by-line, so those classes are healthy. The four broken classes above are the ones that map directly to LongMemEval question types (temporal reasoning, knowledge update, multi-session user facts). Sampled records: mem_d21bf747 (1398B/16 claims), mem_41013645, mem_77a99092 (10.3KB/94), mem_5f15af31 (20.2KB/139), mem_506eff3b (170), mem_07015bc6, mem_9b9b667f, mem_01591b85. raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g4-r0 @ artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex: 47 records, 5246 claims, 28 edges. Claim extraction ran on 100% of records (per-record claim counts match metadata.claims_extracted exactly; range 2-216, ~1 claim/90 chars) and density is balanced across turns (user 10.2 vs assistant 10.5 claims/1k chars), so volume is fine. Quality is the problem: (a) claim subjects frequently degrade to sentence-initial function words ('The', 'It', 'However', 'Here', 'If', 'Now' — top predicates include 'are some', 'here are a few', 'i d be happy'), weakening entity-keyed retrieval even when the fact text is captured in the object; (b) claim_type skew: 4519/5246 are generic 'state', only 107 quantity / 102 event / 186 interval, and quantity objects show a systematic decimal-truncation bug ($8.5M->$8, $180.00->$180); (c) edge extraction produced entity relations for exactly 1 of 47 records. Negation/retraction cues: 28/37 covered — acceptable. Verdict: patchy, driven by quantity corruption, user-narration proper-noun drops, list-item date/attribution loss, and near-zero graph edges.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g5-r0 (52 recs / 4993 claims / 10 edges) has DENSE lexical extraction: every record yields claims (19-224, scaling with length ~10-14 claims/1000 chars), and positional tail-coverage is complete on all sampled records except the telemetry blob (mem_5959679fea254b3e, last 6.2k chars uncovered) -- an apparent 3.4k-char gap in mem_9925979893044148 was a false positive from markdown ** normalization; its tail facts (Rachel Chu event, 'is not the author' retraction, book lists) all have claims. The extractor is a sentence-span shredder: subject=first token, predicate=first-4-words, value=verbatim sentence. So free-read/NLI recall is well fed (claims carry the exact text, including negations like 'Rachel Chu is not the author of \"The Last Leaf\"'), which is why health is 'patchy' rather than worse. But the TYPED layer -- what structured_recall, value_as_of, and deterministic verification consume -- is broken across whole classes: session-timestamp-only valid_at (no relative-date resolution), unit-less unparsed quantities (67/4993), misattributed/junk subjects (only 98 claims subject='user'), zero entity-relation edges, and dropped numeric blobs. Type distribution: state 4308, preference 337, interval 139, event 112, quantity 67, table 30. Store access path: DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data, Engine().store.{all_records,active_claims_at,all_edges}.

Temporal-reasoning questions ('when did I redeem the coupon', before/after ordering, as_of queries) answer against the wrong time axis or not at all — the bi-temporal machinery has nothing to bite on.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: Mr. Johnson is now named Ms. Johnson and is a woman.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The rename produced the tautology [state] 'Ms. Johnson' is named 'Ms. Johnson' plus garbled [state] subj='assistant' pred='is now' obj='now named Ms' (mem_6c5b38a7287f4d02); no alias or supersedes edge from 'Mr. Johnson', so any query keyed to the former name misses the entity entirely. Similarly 'No, the new US $100 bill does not have two pockets...' (mem_ee9d1c45bab14ee7) is stored with no polarity field — negated facts are indistinguishable from positive ones in the typed layer. This defeats the truth-ledger/false-premise and latest-assertion-wins guarantees; verification falls back to NLI, the exact leak in the 2026-07-09 verify-or-abstain headline.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: assistant: No, the new US $100 bill does not have two pockets embedded along the vertical blue line.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: This whole sentence is the `value` of a [quantity] claim with unit='' — and it is typical: of 143 quantity claims, only 41 have numeric values, 38 have units, and 91 carry a sentence (>40 chars) as value. Deterministic aggregation (measure_sum/count paths) reads claim.value, so two-thirds of quantities are either silently dropped or mis-summed; this is precisely the input shape behind the live 5/13 verified-WRONG numeric-aggregation failures (#41/#42).

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g6-r0 (DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data): 47 records, 5,261 active claims, 192 edges with include_inferred=True (168 base — explains the manifest's 192). Sampled 8 records spanning shortest (526 chars, 8 claims) to longest (20,037 chars, 202 claims). Extraction is NOT dead: every record has claims, volume tracks length (~1 claim/90 chars), and substantive sentences survive as proof_atom spans — span-level coverage is healthy, so NLI-based recall over proofs works. The health verdict is patchy because the TYPED layer is class-degraded: (1) ~41% of claims have hash/stopword subjects (top subjects: '58ab32e2_2' x118, 'assistant' x110, 'The' x103); (2) claim-type distribution is 84% generic 'state' (4,404) vs preference 318 / interval 170 / event 157 / quantity 143 / table 69, and many typed claims are mis-typed spans (negations as [quantity], questions as [event]); (3) anchors are empty on 100% of claims and valid_at is session-time not event-time; (4) relationship edges exist for only 11/47 records — 36 records including people-heavy ones (jazz artists, family schedule, caffeine) have zero edges, so graph-join recall covers a quarter of the corpus. Good extractions do exist (e.g. 'DM Arena has 400 m track' value=400 unit=m; 'Ms. Johnson is the school's music teacher' + is-a/serves-as edges with supersedes chains), proving the pipeline can produce clean typed facts — it just does so on a minority of the material. Net: free-read/NLI recall is well-fed; deterministic/verified paths (structured_recall, value_as_of, measure ops, alias-keyed graph queries) are starved, which matches this window's observed behavior.

missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The deterministic path (structured_recall / measure-type sum) has no numeric field to aggregate or compare, so numeric questions fall back to text matching -- this is the exact surface of the known live failure (verified-WRONG over-sums, bc149d6b): verify-or-abstain cannot check arithmetic it never extracted.

Likewise "received a certificate from the platform last month" (mem_9863d45f3e65404c) gets valid_at=2023-05-20 (session ts) even though record.metadata['dates'] contains the resolved 2023-04-01. 0/5184 claims have valid_at differing from their record's valid_at; only 1/29 event_date filters holds a real in-text date (Cyberpunk 2077 -> 2020-12-10), the other 28 echo the session date, mostly triggered by 'get you started' boilerplate.
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: LongMemEval temporal-reasoning questions ('when did the user start/finish X', 'how long between A and B') and value_as_of/fact_history time travel are unanswerable deterministically when every claim inherits the conversation timestamp -- the dates were parsed into record metadata but dropped before the claim layer.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g7-r0 (artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex): 50 records, 5184 claims, 10 edges confirmed. Raw text coverage is DENSE, not dead: every record has claims (2-210, roughly 1 per sentence, proportional to length), all 50 records are claim sources, itemized lists are fully indexed (458 claims carry list_id/list_label/list_size/list_index -- ordinal-list queries are covered), and all 11 real negation/change sentences plus all 4 person-relationship sentences are covered by claim text. So the NLI free-read path sees essentially everything. The patchiness is in the TYPED layer: claim_type distribution is 4353 state / 366 preference / 148 quantity / 123 interval / 118 event / 76 table, with 76% of claims carrying only filters={'untyped':'1'} -- i.e., claims are mostly verbatim sentence wrappers. Quantities have zero parsed values/units, event dates collapse to session time, edges exist for one record only, and subject slots are polluted. Net effect: recall-by-similarity works, but everything deterministic (numeric aggregation, temporal as-of, graph hops, subject-keyed history) is starved -- consistent with the 2026-07-09 live headline that the deterministic path ships verified-wrong numeric answers. Sampled records: mem_71d6a9f3ebff4eb1, mem_8257c79570774b99, mem_2fcf870f2ce64a03, mem_08b929a2d5a6424f, mem_2e44346f971e426c, mem_9863d45f3e65404c, mem_70e194974a1c40b4, mem_15722c691d6c4a26, mem_65956773aac14223. Full record+claim dumps at /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-raunakgengiti-Eidetic-Plus/eb09f70d-9fc9-40be-884b-dc6507a26ca8/scratchpad/sample_dump.txt.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: user: I'm in the process of buying a home with my partner Alex, and we're getting close to closing.

Graph-dependent recall paths (bridge-join, GRAPH_BRIDGE_CONTEXT, linked_memories, value_as_of/fact_history entity time-travel) are all dead in this namespace, so multi-hop and relationship questions can only be answered via raw-text retrieval.

The deterministic measure_sum/aggregation path sums claim values — degenerate values feed exactly the verified-WRONG numeric-aggregation failure class already open as #41/#42.

raw_extraction.zero_extraction_cause: Claims are NOT zero (5207); only the LLM graph channel is dead: edges=0 AND EventRecords=0. Cause: batch window-budget raw-only degrade. Run env set CONSOLIDATION_EXTRACT_CALL_BUDGET=90 with CONSOLIDATION_LONG_HAYSTACK_RAW_ONLY=1; g8's 51 records (many 10-18k chars, chunked at >6000 chars) planned more extraction windows than the 80% budget trigger (72), so eidetic/engine.py:1453-1464 set raw_only_long_haystack=True, remaining_budget=0, window_allowance=0 for every record — client.extract_edges was never called. Evidence: 50/51 g8 records carry metadata consolidation_raw_only='batch_window_budget' and consolidated=False (the 51st also produced 0 triples); sibling namespaces in the same store (g6/g27/g14) have 192/173/152 edges. The 5207 claims come from the budget-exempt deterministic regex extractor claims_for_record in eidetic/smqe/claim_extraction.py, which ran on all 51 records regardless.
raw_extraction.notes: Store: artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data (namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g8-r0). Verified: 51 records, 5207 claims, 0 edges, 0 EventRecords. Every record has claims (min 19, median 101, max 228, proportional to text length); claim types: state 4474, preference 340, interval 150, quantity 123, event 102, table 18. Sampled 7 records (1.6k-18.7k chars). Deterministic sentence-level capture is broad — named facts (Alex, beatbox.studio list items, Phoenix 12 hours, January surge) all appear verbatim in proof_atoms, and negation/'instead of' sentences are captured — so raw-quote recall paths still work; that is why health is 'patchy' not 'dead'. But typed structure is degenerate: subjects are junk tokens ('Financing', 'Add', 'Bring', 'sharegpt_AVnVlaF_0'), quantity/interval values are unparsed whole sentences, and the entire relational/temporal graph layer is absent. Fix options: re-run sleep on this namespace with CONSOLIDATION_LONG_HAYSTACK_RAW_ONLY=0 (or budget raised above the window plan), or use window_cap_per_record path (raw_only off) which guarantees at least 1 window per record; records remain pending-safe since raw text is fully stored (consolidated=False, reprocessable). Comparable check: only 6 sibling namespaces show a suspicious flat 10 edges, all relation 'co_activated' (link_memories at recall time, not extraction) — worth a wider sweep of which groups tripped the same budget degrade.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: By the way, I also stocked up on some winter sweaters at Macy's on November 27th - got them for 50% off, what a steal!
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: The 'November 27th' event date is never parsed: the claim's valid_at is the session ingest timestamp (1685340720 = 2023-05-29). Measured globally, 0 of 51 records have even one claim whose valid_at differs from the record timestamp, and 0 of 4887 claims have invalid_at. Every LongMemEval temporal-reasoning question ('when did I buy X', 'what did I do first') and the bi-temporal machinery (as_of recall, value_as_of, fact_history) is structurally blind on this namespace — event time collapses to session time.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: user: funding from shark tank ₹1 cr for 2% Equity from Aman & Peyush
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: Only 1 of 97 quantity claims has a numeric value field (value=8, unit=percent); the other 96 stuff the entire raw sentence into value with unit=''. The ₹1cr/2% funding fact produced no quantity claim at all (mem_afd5c8ee369f4a3b, 11 claims, 0 quantity), and '50% off' likewise. This is exactly the substrate of the known measure_sum over-sum failure family (1226.3-vs-70, issues #41/#42): deterministic numeric aggregation has no clean numbers to aggregate, so it either abstains or sums garbage.

missed_fact_classes.example_record_quote: funding from shark tank ₹1 cr for 2% Equity from Aman & Peyush
missed_fact_classes.why_it_matters: All 10 edges in the namespace are relation='co_activated' (retrieval provenance links between memory_ids) — zero semantic edges. Investor->company, family relations ('road trip to San Diego with my family'), person->possession never enter the graph, so the entity-relation time-travel path (value_as_of/fact_history) and any multi-hop question ('who invested in the company the user asked about') has no graph to walk; only fuzzy text retrieval remains.

The facts exist in claim text but are absent under any correct key, so entity-scoped structured recall ('what did the USER buy') cannot find them and cross-record identity linking of the same user is impossible.

raw_extraction.notes: Namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g9-r0 (DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data): 51 records, 4887 active claims, 10 edges confirmed. Extraction VOLUME is healthy — 0 zero-claim records, min 11 / median 82 / max 217 claims per record, roughly one claim per sentence with verbatim source text preserved in claim.value; the free-read/NLI retrieval path therefore has material to work with. But the STRUCTURED layer is patchy at the class level: claim types are 4232 state / 292 preference / 161 interval / 105 event / 97 quantity, and the structured fields are mostly degenerate (predicates are first-words-of-sentence like 'by the way i', 'user https hoovufresh com'; quantity value/unit unparsed in 96/97; valid_at always = session time; invalid_at never set, so retraction/supersession is unusable here). Assistant boilerplate ('Congratulations on your amazing deal!', 'Happy shopping') is extracted at the same rate as user facts, diluting the claim set ~2:1 with non-memorable content. Sampled records: mem_afd5c8ee369f4a3b (3626c/11cl, lowest density), mem_247a4e3dba7244e3 (1871c/14cl), mem_84c1b7ffed674706 (12502c/137cl, dated purchases), mem_9479256795ea4350 (11995c/132cl, trip durations/locations), mem_6b5efb81609549e3 (16546c/182cl, itemized cost list), mem_f63d7c1a387542bc (17336c/143cl). Net: recall that quotes raw text can succeed, but every deterministic path (structured_recall, value_as_of, measure_sum aggregation, graph hops) is starved — consistent with the 2026-07-09 headline that the deterministic path ships verified-wrong numeric answers.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Judge file artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/notebooklm_freetier.judged.json scores c7_q41 correct:false, but the FULL nb_answer (artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/notebooklm_freetier.jsonl; the ROW's "specifically a" cutoff is display truncation — bench/notebooklm_freetier_report.py:128 passes the untruncated nb_answer to judge_locomo) explicitly states every gold item ("candles, music, essential oils"): "Deborah uses **candles and essential oils**—specifically a candle with a sprig of rosemary" and a dedicated "**Music:**" bullet ("Deborah finds **instrumental tracks with mellow melodies** helpful... Jolene prefers listening to artists like **Nils Frahm and Olafur Arnalds**"). Gold itself is sound per LoCoMo evidence turns (data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json conv7 q41): D11:4 Deborah "I also bought this candle... to improve my yoga practice"; D11:7 Jolene "I find music helps me"; D28:18 Jolene "I use essential oils and put on some soft, soothing music". Store confirms too: substrate blob 8d30d1439cc7... (= cited mem_52046df942874745) contains "Candles and essential oils add warmth and calm to my yoga session... Do you have any other ways to enhance your yoga practice? Jolene: I find music helps me." The same jsonl row even has prelim_contains_gold:true. The answer conveys the gold as a clearly-stated superset (tolerated); the judge's "no" is the error.

Dataset (data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json, conv idx 4, qa[86]): question="What did John share with the person he skyped about?", gold="Characters from Harry Potter", evidence=["D5:1"]. D5:1 speaker is TIM, not John: "Tim | D5:1 | Hey John! ... Just skyped with that Harry Potter fan I met in CA and had a great time. We talked characters and maybe collab-ing". Grep of all conv-4 sessions: this is the ONLY skype mention — John never skyped anyone. Store record confirmed (artifacts/holdout_rotation_r13_codex/data/eidetic.sqlite, namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g9-r0, mem_c1ddf2dd13de4303): text begins "Tim: Hey John! ... Just skyped with that Harry Potter fan...". Given answer (full row in notebooklm_freetier.jsonl) states the gold substance while correcting the misattribution: "it was **Tim**, rather than John, who mentioned recently using **Skype** to connect with a **Harry Potter fan** ... discussed various **characters** and the possibility of **collaborating**". Judge file notebooklm_freetier.judged.json marks c4_q86 correct=false.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo speaker misattribution: qa[86] of conversation 4 asks what JOHN shared with the person HE skyped, but its own evidence annotation D5:1 is spoken by Tim ("Just skyped with that Harry Potter fan I met in CA... We talked characters"), and no other turn in the conversation mentions Skype. The question is unanswerable as posed for subject John; the gold "Characters from Harry Potter" only matches if the skype call is (wrongly) attributed to John. The model's answer explicitly corrects this and supplies the gold substance, so the judge's correct=false traces to the defective question/gold pair, not to a missing fact in the answer.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Judge row (artifacts/holdout_rotation_r14_codex/notebooklm_freetier.judged.json): {"sample_id":"c4_q7","correct":false}. Full nb_answer (notebooklm_freetier.jsonl) names all three gold locations with correct backing: "**London**: A few years ago, Tim visited **London**... Harry Potter films [1, 2]"; "**California**: He traveled to **California** and met with a fellow Harry Potter fan [3-5]"; "**Smoky Mountains**: Last year, Tim took a trip to the **Smoky Mountains** [2, 6, 7]". Dataset gold evidence (data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json, conv 4 Tim/John, qa[7], evidence D1:18/D3:2/D14:16) are ALL Tim's own turns: D1:18 [Tim] "I went to a place in London a few years ago"; D3:2 [Tim] "I had a nice chat with a Harry Potter fan in California"; D14:16 [Tim] "I snapped that pic on my trip to the Smoky Mountains last year". Superset extras are also dataset-true for Tim, not hallucinations: D13:1 [Tim] "Last week I went to a Harry Potter conference in the UK"; D18:1 [Tim] "I went to a castle during my trip to the UK last Friday"; D28:1/28:3 [Tim] Ireland study-abroad in Galway (only a minor tense overreach: "stayed in Galway" vs planned; conversation ends session 29 pre-departure). Future-only places (NYC, Italy, Paris...) are correctly segregated as planned. Answer states the gold fact verbatim as an accurate superset; judge's false is a miscall.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: No gold defect: all three evidence turns are spoken by Tim (the question's subject). If anything, gold is incomplete — Tim's own turns D13:1 (Harry Potter conference in the UK) and D18:1 (castle trip in the UK) are additional places Tim has been that gold omits — but this does not harm the adjudication since the answer contains the gold set exactly.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold evidence D12:21 is Tim's turn but only says "The Wolves are solid and LeBron's skills and leadership are amazing." — a compliment, not a fandom claim. The explicit declaration is John's: D12:20 "Thanks! The Wolves are my team for sure." — answering Tim's question TO John (D12:19 "Cool! Who's your favorite basketball team/player?"). John plays for the Wolves (D1:5 "The Minnesota Wolves! I can't wait to play with them!"). No turn in all of conv-43 has Tim naming a team he supports (checked every Tim turn; closest is D9:5 "Nope, never been on a sports team. I'm more into reading and fantasy novels."). Store (29 records, namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g0-r0) contains exactly these two Wolves passages, matching the dataset. The given answer saw and correctly attributed them: "Tim's friend, John, plays for and supports the Minnesota Wolves... Tim remarked that the Wolves are 'solid'" — but headlined "no specific mention of a basketball team that Tim supports," which contradicts the gold, so the judge (correct=false) applied the gold faithfully; not a judge error. Gold's reading (agreeing a team is "solid" in a favorites chat = support, esp. one's friend's team) is a defensible conversational-implicature reading; the model's strict abstention with correct attribution is equally defensible.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: locomo10.json conv-43 qa[113] (c4_q113): gold "The Wolves" is annotator inference, not stated fact. The cited evidence D12:21 (Tim: "The Wolves are solid...") does not assert support; the only "my team" assertion in the evidence context is D12:20, spoken by John (the question's subject is Tim), and John is a Wolves *player*, not merely a fan. Not the clean wrong-speaker defect (D12:21 is Tim's turn), so classified ambiguous rather than defective.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold (locomo10.json conv-48 qa[44], category 2 temporal): "on 9 April, 2023", evidence D12:1. Given answer's FIRST sentence: "Deborah attended an art show with a friend on **April 9, 2023** [1, 2]" — exact paraphrase of the gold date, never retracted. Judge row (notebooklm_freetier.judged.json c7_q44): "correct": false, despite run-row prelim_contains_gold=true. The trailing caveat is source-faithful: dataset evidence turn D12:1 (Deborah, 4:30 pm on 9 April, 2023): "Had a blast biking nearby with my neighbor last week ... Checked out an art show with a friend today" — Anna is never named at the art show anywhere in conv-48 (she is "my new neighbor Anna", D3:4, 1 Feb 2023). Store record mem_a8454773640b4e9f (c7_session_12, valid_at=2023-04-09T23:30Z) contains the identical turn, and no store record links Anna to the art show. For a temporal question whose gold is only a date, an answer that leads with the exact gold date plus a truthful hedge states the gold fact; the judge miscalibrated by failing it.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: Secondary (not the verdict): the question's presupposition "with Anna" is an annotator inference — evidence D12:1 says only "an art show with a friend", and the same turn separately calls Anna "my neighbor" (biking, prior week), so the friend=Anna link is nowhere explicit in conv-48. This under-supported presupposition is exactly why the model's hedge was faithful, but the gold DATE itself is correct and unambiguous.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Dataset (data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json, conv-44 = c5, qa[81], category 4): question "What did Andrew and his GF do on the Monday before July 24, 2023?", gold "volunteered at a pet shelter", evidence D13:1. Session 13 is dated "3:52 pm on 27 July, 2023" (a Thursday), and D13:1 is Andrew: "My GF and I just had a great experience volunteering at a pet shelter on Monday" -- i.e. the Monday before the July 27 session = Monday, July 24, 2023. Calendar check: 2023-07-24 IS a Monday; "the Monday before July 24, 2023" is therefore July 17, a date on which nothing is recorded anywhere in the conversation. Store confirms the only shelter fact: mem_c85c4e12e2ef4ec6 (source c5_session_13, valid_at 2023-07-27 15:52): "Andrew: ... volunteering at a pet shelter on Monday". The model's answer contains the gold verbatim with the correct date resolution -- "on **Monday, July 24, 2023**, Andrew and his girlfriend **volunteered at a pet shelter** [1, 2]" -- and correctly reports no recorded activity for July 17.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: The question's temporal anchor is a LoCoMo generation artifact: the template "the <weekday> before <date>" was instantiated with the resolved EVENT date (July 24) instead of the session date (July 27). Since July 24, 2023 is itself a Monday, "the Monday before July 24" literally denotes July 17, 2023, while the gold answer's event (per its own evidence turn D13:1, session dated 27 July 2023) happened ON July 24. The gold label "volunteered at a pet shelter" is thus false for the question as worded; only "the Monday before July 27, 2023" would make gold+evidence consistent. The model answered the defective question more accurately than the gold: it abstained for July 17 and supplied the pet-shelter fact for the correct date, July 24.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Dataset conv-47 qa[64] gold="nearly four months", evidence ["D17:1","D30:2","D30:4"]. D17:1 (session_17, "9:49 am on 22 July, 2022"): John: "I just started playing chess to get better at strategy." D30:2 (session_30, "5:20 pm on 5 November, 2022"): John: "I had a great accomplishment this Tuesday!" D30:4: John: "I won the regional chess tournament." Nov 5, 2022 was a Saturday, so "this Tuesday" = Nov 1, 2022. Jul 22 -> Nov 1 = 102 days = 3.35 months; Jul 22 -> Nov 5 = 106 days = 3.48 months. The r14 store (namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g3-r0) contains both turns with correct event times (2022-07-22, 2022-11-05). The model's answer "approximately three and a half months" matches the interval computed from the gold's own evidence turns; the gold "nearly four months" does not (4 months would land Nov 22, 2.5+ weeks later).
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo annotation arithmetic error: the gold "nearly four months" is inconsistent with its own cited evidence. Start pinned by D17:1 ("just started playing chess", 22 July 2022); win pinned by D30:2+D30:4 ("this Tuesday" relative to 5 November 2022, a Saturday => 1 November 2022). True duration is 102-106 days = 3.35-3.48 months, i.e. "about three and a half months" -- exactly what the model answered. "Nearly four months" overstates the interval by ~2.5-3 weeks; the annotator likely coarse-counted July->November as four months. The judge correctly matched against the label, but the label itself is defective.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Dataset evidence turn D20:4 (locomo10.json conv c9, speaker Calvin — correct subject): "I had an amazing drive one summer day. The wind blowing through my hair and the rush of freedom - it was unforgettable. I've pondered the intricacies of life, taking a moment to truly appreciate the path I've chosen..." Gold "feeling the wind blowing through his hair" is one conjunct of that sentence; the given answer states the OTHER conjuncts from the same sentence ("pondering the intricacies of life", "reflect on and appreciate the path he had chosen", "rush of freedom") plus the session-5 record ("long drives... help me relax... wind and the open road... clear my head", confirmed verbatim in store record c9_session_5; D20:4 confirmed verbatim in store record c9_session_20). The answer never states the wind/hair fact, so it is not a paraphrase/superset of gold (answer_correct_judge_wrong fails); but the question "What activity did Calvin enjoy during his summer drives?" admits multiple grounded answers from the single evidence turn, and "pondering the intricacies of life" is the only literal activity listed (wind-in-hair is a sensation). Gold is correctly attributed and supported, so not defective; both readings are defensible.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: Not a defect in gold or attribution. The ambiguity is in the question: evidence turn D20:4 lists several enjoyed elements of the same summer drive (wind in hair, rush of freedom, pondering the intricacies of life), and the gold arbitrarily selects the sensation ("feeling the wind blowing through his hair") while the question word "activity" better matches the co-listed "pondered the intricacies of life" that the model chose.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold (locomo10.json conv 9, qa 7): question "Does Dave's shop employ a lot of people?", answer "Yes", sole evidence D4:17. D4:17 (Dave, attribution correct): "This is a photo of my shop. Come by sometime, if you can!" + blip_caption "a photo of a group of people standing in front of a car" (image query: "car maintenance shop grand opening"). No headcount exists anywhere in the conversation; the "Yes" rests entirely on this caption, and the pictured people are never identified as employees. The store (namespace eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g8-r0) contains this turn verbatim, and the answer cited it explicitly: "'group of people standing in front of a car,' suggesting several individuals are associated with the business [3]" — then concluded "it is clear he has a staff, [but] the sources do not characterize the size of the team as being 'a lot' or provide a specific headcount." The judge (correct:false) applied the gold as written correctly, since the answer never affirms "Yes" to the "a lot" question. Both readings are defensible: gold's loose common-sense inference (group photo of a shop = lots of staff) fits LoCoMo category-3 open-domain style, while the model's "team confirmed, 'a lot' unverifiable" is an epistemically sound reading of the identical evidence. Neither answer_correct_judge_wrong (the answer withholds the gold fact) nor gold_label_defective (evidence speaker/subject are correct; gold is under-determined, not provably wrong) applies.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: Not a hard defect, but the gold "Yes" for a subjective quantifier ("a lot") is supported only by an image caption ("a group of people standing in front of a car") whose subjects are not stated to be employees — the underlying stock photo's retrieval query was "car maintenance shop grand opening", so the group could be customers/visitors. Any system that verifies before asserting will abstain on "a lot" and be scored wrong despite citing the exact gold evidence turn.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold (data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json, conv idx 2, qa idx 32): "Hiking, mountaineering". The full judged answer (artifacts/holdout_rotation_r14_codex/notebooklm_freetier.jsonl, nb_answer for c2_q32) states both gold facts verbatim as its first two bullets: "**Hiking:** John went on a hiking trip with his workmates" and "**Mountaineering:** He also went on a mountaineering trip with workmates to help \"clear his head\" [3]. He successfully **reached the summit**". Both facts are genuinely in the dataset spoken by John himself — D16:1 John: "I got this picture of my workmates when we went on a hiking trip"; D18:2 John: "I went on a mountaineering trip last week with some workmates" — and both are in the store (mem_f8a8ddf8c7aa46b7 hiking; mem_3c6e7212c7744047 mountaineering, John's turn inside the session-18 record). The answer is a superset (adds yoga, which is itself dataset-supported: "I started a weekend yoga class with a colleague", photo practicing outdoors), and superset is tolerated per rubric. Yet notebooklm_freetier.judged.json rows has {"sample_id": "c2_q32", "correct": false} — the pinned judge scored a gold-superset answer wrong. Note: the dataset's evidence annotation D16:2 points at Maria's reply instead of John's D16:1 hiking turn (off-by-one), but the gold label itself is correct, so this is not a gold defect.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: Minor annotation nit only (does not change the verdict): evidence pointer "D16:2" is Maria's reply; the actual hiking statement is D16:1 (John). Gold answer text itself is correct and fully supported.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Dataset (/Users/raunakgengiti/Eidetic-Plus/data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json, conv-50 = c9, qa[137]): question "When did Calvin first get interested in cars?", gold "at an early age", evidence ["D26:6"]. But D26:6 is spoken by DAVE, not Calvin: "D26:6 | Dave | Thanks, Calvin! It's always been my thing. My dad took me to my first car show when I was 10, and I was wow-ed! I was hooked...". Exhaustive scan of all 50 Calvin car-mentioning turns in conv-50 finds zero that date his interest; his only origin-adjacent line is D26:5 "I'm totally into cars too and love making new and powerful models" (no timeframe). The only age-adjacent car-origin turns in the whole conversation (D12:4 "working on cars with my dad as a kid", D26:6) are both Dave's. Store check (DATA_DIR=artifacts/holdout_rotation_r15_codex/data, ns eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g7-r0, 30 records): the D26:6 turn is in-store verbatim with the "Dave:" speaker prefix (mem_8ef13dad0b8e4ef2), and no record attributes an early-age car interest to Calvin. The given answer -- "The sources do not provide a specific timeframe or age for when Calvin first became interested in cars" -- is therefore the faithful reading; it was judged wrong only against a gold that transplants Dave's childhood story onto Calvin.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo conv-50 qa[137] (category 4, answerable single-hop): gold answer "at an early age" with evidence annotation D26:6 is a speaker misattribution. D26:6 is a Dave turn ("My dad took me to my first car show when I was 10... I was hooked") answering Calvin's question in D26:5 about how DAVE got into engineering cars. The annotator lifted Dave's origin story as the answer to a question about Calvin. The conversation contains no turn in which Calvin dates his own interest in cars, so the question is unanswerable as posed and the correct behavior is exactly the abstention the model produced.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold (locomo10.json conv-50 qa#64): "weekly visits to local parks, countryside roadtrip, celebration of the opening of his car maintenance shop, card-playing nights"; all 4 evidence turns (D10:3, D11:1, D6:8, D15:1) are spoken by Dave, so the gold is clean. The full nb_answer in artifacts/holdout_rotation_r15_codex/notebooklm_freetier.jsonl (the exact field judge_rows feeds the judge, bench/notebooklm_freetier_report.py:128) states every gold fact: "road trip with his friends to see the 'stunning countryside'" [roadtrip]; "arranged for regular walks with friends in the park" [weekly park visits]; "enjoyed a card-night with his friends" [card-playing nights]; "when he opened his own car maintenance shop, he invited friends over to celebrate" [shop-opening celebration]. The row's given_answer was merely display-truncated at "recharge and". The judged sidecar (notebooklm_freetier.judged.json) scores c9_q64 correct=false; re-running the pinned qwen3-max judge (temp 0.0, judge_locomo prompt "allowing paraphrase") on the identical full answer reproduces False 3/3, while an answer containing ONLY the same four facts is judged True — the judge is dinging the grounded superset content (rock-band jamming/logo bullets), which the rubric explicitly tolerates. The extra activities are themselves store-supported (eidetic verified_claims: "Dave was jamming with his band"), not fabrications.

Raw LoCoMo (data/bench/locomo/locomo10.json, conv idx 7, qa[116]): question "According to Jolene, what does exercise help her to feel?", gold "connected to her body", evidence ["D7:20"] — but D7:20 is spoken by Deborah: "Exercise is key for me - it makes me feel connected to my body." (speaker_a=Deborah, speaker_b=Jolene; D7:19 Jolene: "Why did you decide that?", D7:21 Jolene: "This is a great healthy habit!"). Store record mem_64a92db3aa5545e0 (ns eidetic-plus-full-locomo-g2-r0) preserves the same attribution: "Jolene: Why did you decide that?\nDeborah: Exercise is key for me - it makes me feel connected to my body." Jolene never says the gold phrase anywhere; her actual exercise statements are D7:3 "I chill out and gain perspective when I do yoga", D7:13 "helps me feel calmer and clearer, so I'm able to handle challenges more easily", D16:10 "yoga and meditation... stay balanced and grounded" — exactly what the given answer states.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo conv-48 (locomo10.json idx 7) qa[116] misattributes Deborah's utterance to Jolene: the question's subject is Jolene but the sole evidence turn D7:20 ("Exercise is key for me - it makes me feel connected to my body.") is Deborah's line, and the gold answer "connected to her body" is derived from it. A model faithful to speaker attribution cannot match this gold; the given answer correctly reports Jolene's own statements (calmer/clearer, chill out, perspective, grounded).

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold evidence pointer D26:6 (locomo10.json, conv-50/c9, qa[137]) is spoken by DAVE, not Calvin: "Thanks, Calvin! It's always been my thing. raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo speaker-attribution error: gold answer "at an early age" for "When did Calvin first get interested in cars?" derives from evidence turn D26:6, which is Dave's autobiographical statement ("My dad took me to my first car show when I was 10... I was hooked"), immediately following Calvin asking Dave "What got you into engineering cars?" (D26:5). raw_adjudications.evidence: The judged answer (full 1704-char nb_answer in artifacts/holdout_rotation_r15_codex/notebooklm_freetier_run1.jsonl; the error row for c9_q64 is filtered out by judge_rows' `if "error" not in r` in bench/notebooklm_freetier_report.py, so the judge saw this text) explicitly states all 4 gold items: "**road trip with his friends**, where they explored the 'stunning countryside'" (= countryside roadtrip); "enjoyed a **card-night** with his friends" (= card-playing nights); "arranged **regular walks with friends in the park**" (= weekly visits to local parks); "opening his own **car maintenance shop**, Dave **invited friends over to celebrate**" (= celebration of shop opening). raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo locomo10.json conv-48 (Deborah/Jolene), qa index 116, category 4 (single-hop): question subject is "Jolene" but the sole evidence turn D7:20 belongs to speaker Deborah ("Exercise is key for me - it makes me feel connected to my body."). Gold answer "connected to her body" is Deborah's fact misattributed to Jolene; the closest Jolene utterance (D30:6 "connected to nature and myself", about yoga at a sunrise retreat) is neither the gold phrasing nor the annotated evidence. raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold is valid: locomo10.json conv-42 (c3) evidence turn D9:9 is Joanna herself: "I really enjoy dramas and emotionally-driven films." But the answer states the gold fact by paraphrase+superset: it opens "Joanna is primarily drawn to **dramas and romcoms**, as she loves becoming **immersed in the feelings and plots**" (verbatim from Joanna's own D1:14: "I'm all about dramas and romcoms. I love getting immersed in the feelings and plots.") and adds "She views films as emotionally powerful tools that can... evoke deep emotions" and "Romantic Dramas: One of her favorite movies is a romantic drama" — i.e., dramas explicitly + emotionally-driven by direct paraphrase. The answer's citation [2] (reference index 1 in notebooklm_freetier_run2.jsonl, attached to that first sentence) literally contains the gold as a claim line: "enjoys -> dramas and emotionally-driven films [eidetic:mem_5000780f388b]". Extra genres (romcoms, superhero "sucker for any superhero", LOTR) are all Joanna's own statements — a tolerated superset. Yet notebooklm_freetier_run2.judged.json has {"sample_id": "c3_q115", "correct": false}. Judge scored a paraphrase/superset hit as wrong.
raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: No dataset defect: gold evidence turn D9:9 is correctly attributed to Joanna. Note only that the conversation contains two verbatim Joanna self-descriptions of her film taste (D9:9 "dramas and emotionally-driven films" and D1:14 "dramas and romcoms... immersed in the feelings and plots"); the gold annotates one, the model led with the other, and the two are near-synonymous from the same speaker.

raw_adjudications.evidence: Gold evidence anchor D26:6 (locomo10.json conv-50, qa idx 137) is DAVE's turn, not Calvin's: "Dave: Thanks, Calvin! It's always been my thing. My dad took me to my first car show when I was 10, and I was wow-ed!" The immediately preceding turn D26:5 is Calvin asking Dave "What got you into engineering cars? I'm totally into cars too and love making new and powerful models" — with no timeframe for Calvin. Finally owning a luxury car was a dream come true"). The D20:6 "road trip with my dad" is about a song, not cars. raw_adjudications.dataset_defect_detail: LoCoMo speaker mis-attribution in conv-50 QA #137: the question asks about Calvin, but the gold answer "at an early age" and its sole evidence anchor D26:6 describe Dave's origin story ("My dad took me to my first car show when I was 10" — spoken by Dave). No turn by Calvin anywhere in the conversation supports "at an early age" for Calvin. The gold answer is only true of Dave; as annotated for Calvin it is unsupported by the dataset itself, making the label defective rather than the model wrong.

Avoid using harsh chemicals or acidic substances [10]"; every section (disposal, faucet, utensil holder, granite) maps to the user's own stored sessions (grounding: 10/10 high-overlap quotes, 0.943 answer token coverage). Store verified live (DATA_DIR=artifacts/lme_s_r1_codex/data, namespace eidetic-plus-full-longmemeval-g3-r0, 46 records): "I recently bought a new utensil holder to keep countertops clutter-free" and "I noticed some scratches on my granite countertop near the sink" both present in one session record. Judge scored correct:false (notebooklm_freetier.judged.json) despite the answer meeting every rubric element; the sole blemish is the hedge "If you have granite countertops" instead of asserting it, which does not negate the substantive match.
